name: Renegade
originator: Jalaiah Harmon
originator_story: Jalaiah Harmon, a formally trained dancer from Fayetteville, Georgia, choreographed the Renegade in her bedroom at age 14 on Sept 25, 2019, and first posted it on Funimate and Instagram. The dance exploded on TikTok via other accounts (notably @global.jones) and made stars of white creators like Charli D'Amelio and Addison Rae, while Harmon went almost entirely uncredited. She became the central example of Black girls' viral choreography being lifted without attribution, until a Feb 13, 2020 New York Times profile by Taylor Lorenz, 'The Original Renegade,' established her authorship.
timeline: Choreographed and posted on Funimate/Instagram Sept 25 2019 -> reposted and spread on TikTok Oct 2019, popularized by Charli D'Amelio and Addison Rae without crediting Harmon -> peak virality Dec 2019-Feb 2020 -> NYT profile (Taylor Lorenz) Feb 13 2020 restores credit -> K Camp publicly thanks her, she performs at NBA All-Star Game Feb 16 2020.
fun_facts: Canonical case of an uncredited Black teen creator. Harmon developed the routine partly from a friend (Kaliyah Davis) collaboration and built it from existing moves (the Woah, the Wave). Sparked the broader 'who owns a viral dance' / credit-for-Black-creators conversation; she later got a Togethxr docuseries ('I Am: Jalaiah') and a Sufjan Stevens music video cameo.

name: Savage
originator: Keara Wilson
originator_story: Keara Wilson, an Ohio native (20 at the time), choreographed the Savage dance to Megan Thee Stallion's 'Savage' in March 2020, telling Cosmopolitan it took her about one hour. The #SavageChallenge became one of the defining dances of the COVID lockdown. In July 2021 Wilson became one of the first BIPOC TikTok creators to formally copyright her choreography (via labanotation) through choreographer JaQuel Knight's foundation and Logitech, giving her credit, monetization rights, and legal standing.
timeline: Choreographed to 'Savage' March 2020 -> #SavageChallenge goes viral during lockdown spring 2020, helping push the song and the BeyoncE remix -> Wilson formally copyrights the choreography July 2021 as part of JaQuel Knight x Logitech BIPOC creator initiative.
fun_facts: Megan Thee Stallion herself performed Wilson's choreography. The copyright milestone was significant: the US Copyright Office receives fewer than 20 choreography applications a year. Wilson was honored alongside other Black creators whose dances had gone uncredited, part of the same 2021 reckoning that included a Black-TikTok-creator strike refusing to choreograph Megan's later single 'Thot Shit.' Note: a separate 'Savage Remix' dance (Beyonce feature) was created by the Nae Nae Twins.

name: Say So
originator: Haley Sharpe
originator_story: Haley Sharpe (@yodelinghaley), a teenage dancer from Huntsville, Alabama (17 at the time), created the Say So dance and posted it to TikTok on Dec 11, 2019, single-handedly turning a Hot Pink album track into a viral hit and eventual #1. Unlike the Renegade case, Sharpe was credited: Doja Cat invited her to cameo in the official 'Say So' music video (Feb 27, 2020), where Doja performs Sharpe's routine, and influencers like Charli D'Amelio consistently tagged her.
timeline: Sharpe posts the dance Dec 11 2019 (full version shortly after) -> goes viral on TikTok late 2019-early 2020, carried further by Charli D'Amelio and James Charles -> Doja Cat releases official 'Say So' video Feb 27 2020 featuring Sharpe's cameo and choreography -> song hits Billboard Hot 100 #1 (May 2020 with Nicki Minaj remix).
fun_facts: Held up as the positive counterexample to Renegade: Doja Cat publicly crediting and including the teen creator was widely praised precisely because the Jalaiah Harmon controversy had just happened. The routine's signature 'punch and roll' move maps to the lyric 'no punches left to roll with.' Note: a separate live performance of 'Say So' at the 2020 Billboard Music Awards was choreographed by Fullout Cortland, distinct from Sharpe's original TikTok dance.

name: The Git Up
originator: Blanco Brown
originator_story: Blanco Brown (born Bennie Amey III) wrote, produced, and choreographed 'The Git Up' himself, then seeded the craze by filming a no-frills line dance in his foyer and posting a step-by-step tutorial. Here the song artist and the dance originator are the same person, and the routine is largely self-explaining because Brown calls the moves ('two-step, then the cowboy boogie') in the lyrics.
timeline: Released on SoundCloud Apr 26 2019 with a self-performed line-dance clip; official single May 3 2019 -> Brown posts a four-step tutorial mid-May 2019 -> #TheGitUpChallenge explodes on TikTok and YouTube summer 2019 (UK teen Harvey Bass's 'I got suspended for this' clip a key viral moment, later featured in the official video) -> tops Billboard Hot Country Songs for 12 weeks from July 13 2019 and reaches the Hot 100 top 20.
fun_facts: Framed as the 'sequel' to Lil Nas X's 'Old Town Road' in the 2019 country-rap / 'trailer trap' viral wave. Brown built the track from a borrowed lap steel guitar plus spoons, tambourine, and beatboxing, and dedicated it to his grandmother's mantra ('Get up. Go do something productive'). The word 'Git Up' is never actually sung in the song.

name: Up
originator: Mya Nicole Johnson and Chris Cotter
originator_story: Mya Nicole Johnson (15) and Chris Cotter (13), two Black teenagers from Texas, choreographed the 'Up' dance to Cardi B's single in February 2021, telling Distractify it took them about 15 minutes. Cardi B reposted Johnson's original to her own TikTok and Instagram. The dance became a flashpoint when Addison Rae performed it (and seven other dances by mostly Black creators) on The Tonight Show with Jimmy Fallon without on-air credit, triggering national backlash.

celebrity_performances.performer: Addison Rae (performed uncredited on The Tonight Show)

timeline: Johnson and Cotter post the 'Up' dance Feb 2021 -> goes viral (Johnson's original ~8M views; Cardi B's repost ~14.5M) -> Addison Rae performs it on The Tonight Show Mar 26 2021 with no on-air credit -> backlash over uncredited Black creators -> Fallon brings Johnson, Cotter and other creators on the show Apr 5 2021 to perform and discuss their dances.
fun_facts: Central case in the March-April 2021 Addison Rae / Jimmy Fallon credit controversy. Side-by-side clips showed the originals were sharper and more energetic than the televised version. Johnson told Teen Vogue it was not the first time this had happened to her and that she did not want it to keep happening to Black creators. Rae told TMZ the dancers were 'all credited in the original YouTube posting' but that crediting live was 'kinda hard.'

name: Corvette Corvette
originator: Dorien Scott
originator_story: The 'Corvette Corvette' dance to Popp Hunna's song was created by Dorien Scott and became a major TikTok challenge. Scott was among the creators (most of them Black) whose dances Addison Rae performed without on-air credit on The Tonight Show in March 2021, and was one of the creators Jimmy Fallon later invited onto the show to perform and be acknowledged.

celebrity_performances.performer: Addison Rae (performed uncredited on The Tonight Show)

timeline: Popp Hunna releases 'Corvette Corvette' (late 2020) -> Dorien Scott's dance goes viral on TikTok around early 2021 -> performed uncredited by Addison Rae on The Tonight Show Mar 26 2021 -> Scott credited and invited onto the show Apr 5 2021 amid the broader backlash. (Exact origin date and original-video URL not yet verified.)
fun_facts: Part of the same March-April 2021 Tonight Show credit controversy as 'Up,' 'Savage,' and 'Blinding Lights.' Attribution here rests on multiple journalism roundups of the Fallon segment rather than a dedicated creator profile; treat the precise origin date as unconfirmed pending deeper research.

name: Blinding Lights
originator: Adam Snyder, Nate Nale and Greg Dahl (for the routine popularized via The Tonight Show)
originator_story: The Weeknd's 'Blinding Lights' spawned one of the largest dance challenges of 2020. The specific routine that Addison Rae performed on The Tonight Show in March 2021 was credited by NBC to Adam Snyder, Nate Nale and Greg Dahl, who were later invited onto the show. Because 'Blinding Lights' generated multiple distinct viral routines worldwide, attribution of 'the' Blinding Lights dance is genuinely contested.

celebrity_performances.performer: Addison Rae (performed uncredited on The Tonight Show)

timeline: The Weeknd releases 'Blinding Lights' Nov 2019 -> multiple dance challenges spread through 2020 (often family/quarantine versions) -> the routine Rae performed is credited to Snyder, Nale and Dahl, who appear on The Tonight Show Apr 5 2021. (Origin date and original-video URL unconfirmed; needs dedicated research to separate the competing routines.)
fun_facts: 'Blinding Lights' became the longest-charting song in Billboard Hot 100 history, and its dance challenges were correspondingly sprawling. Caution: unlike single-author dances such as Renegade, there is no one undisputed 'Blinding Lights dance' originator; the names here are specifically the creators NBC credited for the Tonight Show routine.

name: Laxed (Siren Beat) / Savage Love
originator: Jawsh 685 (Joshua Nanai) — beat; viral dance was communal, no single choreographer
originator_story: Jawsh 685 (Joshua Christian Nanai, b. 4 Nov 2002, South Auckland NZ; Samoan/Cook Islands descent) made the 'Laxed (Siren Beat)' instrumental in about four hours on a broken laptop, drawing on the NZ Polynesian 'siren jam' scene. Jason Derulo teased 'Savage Love' over the beat in May 2020 WITHOUT clearance or initial credit, sparking cultural-appropriation backlash; they later struck a deal crediting Jawsh as co-artist and sole producer. The viral TikTok #culturedance trend to it was communal with no single named choreographer.
song: Laxed (Siren Beat) / Savage Love (Jawsh 685 & Jason Derulo)
timeline: Beat uploaded to YouTube July 2019 -> early-mid 2020 TikTok #culturedance virality -> May 2020 Derulo 'Savage Love' version -> US #1 via BTS remix Oct 2020.
fun_facts: Jawsh became the first Pasifika and third New Zealander to top the UK singles chart. Do NOT confuse with Keara Wilson's 'Savage' dance for Megan Thee Stallion's different song 'Savage.'

name: Toosie Slide
originator: Toosie (Atlanta), with Ayo & Teo and Hiii Key
originator_story: Drake commissioned the dance: he texted Atlanta dancer Toosie asking for a routine to an unfinished hook, and the crew (at Ayo & Teo's house) pieced it together and sent a rough video back in under 45 minutes. The song and dance are named after Toosie. Explicitly manufactured/seeded — the crew posted it ~29 March 2020, five days before release.
timeline: ~29 March 2020 seed video -> 31 March Drake claims it -> 3 April 2020 release -> US #1 (week ending 14 May 2020).
fun_facts: Drake's first US #1 dance record; compared to Cha Cha Slide/Cupid Shuffle/Electric Slide. Over 4.5M TikToks and 7.6B views by July 2021. Pitchfork/The Ringer panned it as a business decision.

name: Wednesday dance / Bloody Mary
originator: Jenna Ortega
originator_story: Jenna Ortega choreographed the Rave'N dance for Netflix's Wednesday (S1E4, 2022) herself, two nights before filming, citing Siouxsie Sioux, Bob Fosse's Rich Man's Frug, Lene Lovich, Denis Lavant and 1980s goth-club footage. Critical song-swap: the scene uses The Cramps' 'Goo Goo Muck' (1981), but on TikTok fans swapped in a sped-up Lady Gaga 'Bloody Mary' (2011), which drove that song up the charts 11 years later.
song: Goo Goo Muck (The Cramps) in-show; Bloody Mary (Lady Gaga) on TikTok
timeline: 23 Nov 2022 premiere -> late Nov 2022 TikTok dance + Bloody Mary audio swap -> Gaga recreates it -> 'Bloody Mary' sent to radio Dec 2022-Jan 2023.
fun_facts: Ortega: 'I choreographed that myself and I think it's very obvious that I'm not a dancer.' Recreated ~1.5M times within weeks. Gaga later wrote 'The Dead Dance' for Wednesday S2 (2025).

name: Apple (the Apple dance)
originator: Kelley Heyer
originator_story: Kelley Heyer, a NYC-based actor and TikTok creator, posted the 'Apple' dance on 14-15 June 2024, calling it Brat's most underappreciated song. It became a defining 'Brat summer' trend; Charli XCX brought Heyer onstage during the SWEAT tour ('She made the fucking dance, bitch'). Heyer filed a choreography copyright application (30 Aug 2024) and licensed the dance to Fortnite ('Bratty' emote, Dec 2024) and Netflix.
timeline: 14-15 June 2024 dance -> summer 2024 virality (tied to 'Brat summer') -> Aug 2024 Roblox/copyright friction -> Dec 2024 Fortnite deal -> April 2025 Roblox lawsuit -> 2025 settlement.
fun_facts: Modern choreography-IP test case: Heyer sued Roblox (filed 11 April 2025) alleging it earned ~$123,000 selling the dance as an emote (Aug-Nov 2024) without a signed deal; Roblox said it had agreed to license for $9,000. Amicably resolved 2025. NOTE: the genuinely viral 'Brat' dance is Apple, not 'Von Dutch.'

name: Anxiety
originator: No single documented choreographer (based on a ~1990 Fresh Prince scene)
originator_story: Doechii's 'Anxiety' (originally a 2019 YouTube demo; officially released 4 March 2025; samples Gotye's 'Somebody That I Used to Know') went viral via a TikTok trend built on a ~1990 Fresh Prince of Bel-Air scene of Will Smith copying cousin Ashley (Tatyana Ali). No reputable outlet identifies a single originating choreographer; Will Smith's own recreation credited 'Ib: @Mimii' in his caption, but @Mimii's real name is not documented — treat as effectively uncredited.

celebrity_performances.performer: cast of The White Lotus S3

timeline: 2019 YouTube demo -> 2023 Sleepy Hallow sample -> ~Feb 2025 TikTok trend -> 4 March 2025 official release (#9 Hot 100, Doechii's first top 10) -> mid-March 2025 Will Smith/Tatyana Ali recreation.
fun_facts: Rare case where a decades-old TV scene became the choreography. Named TikTok's music trend of the year for 2025.

name: Gangnam Style
originator: Lee Ju-sun (choreographer)
originator_story: PSY's 'Gangnam Style' horse-riding dance was created by choreographer Lee Ju-sun; PSY spent ~30 nights trying animal-inspired moves before Lee landed the horse-riding move 'only in five minutes.' Credit/compensation dispute: per The Korea Times, PSY earned an estimated $92M while Lee was paid a 'minuscule' lump-sum percentage; Lee campaigned for a state body to protect choreographers' copyrights.
timeline: 15 July 2012 release -> viral August 2012 -> first YouTube video to hit 1B views.
fun_facts: UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon called it a possible 'force for world peace.' Lee later debuted as a singer ('DRUWA').

name: Harlem Shake (meme)
originator: George Miller/Filthy Frank (format); The Sunny Coast Skate (viral template)
originator_story: The 2013 Harlem Shake MEME format was established by George Miller/Filthy Frank (30 Jan 2013) and cemented by Australian teens The Sunny Coast Skate (2 Feb 2013): quiet intro, bass drop, costumed chaos. CRITICAL distinction: this is NOT the authentic Harlem shake, a Harlem street dance dating to ~1981 (traced to dancer Al B / Albert Boyce); Harlem residents objected that the meme 'is not the Harlem Shake at all.'
timeline: 30 Jan 2013 Filthy Frank -> 2 Feb 2013 Sunny Coast Skate -> ~4,000 videos/day by 10 Feb -> song debuted Hot 100 #1 on 21 Feb 2013 (after Billboard added YouTube data) -> 1B views by 24 March 2013.
fun_facts: Baauer's track samples Plastic Little ('do the Harlem shake') and Hector Delgado. Baauer later took legal action over an unauthorized political use.

name: Watch Me (Whip/Nae Nae)
originator: Silentó (song); moves pre-existing — Nae Nae from We Are Toonz
originator_story: Silentó (Richard 'Ricky' Hawk, Atlanta) did not invent the moves; he combined pre-existing dances. The Nae Nae is credited to Atlanta group We Are Toonz ('Drop That #NaeNae,' 2013), inspired by Martin Lawrence's 'Sheneneh' character; the Whip was an existing move. Critics noted Silentó 'lazily capitalized' on existing trends.
timeline: 2013 We Are Toonz Nae Nae -> Jan 2015 Silentó SoundCloud -> DanceOn #WatchMeDanceOn campaign April 2015 -> viral June 2015, #3 Hot 100, 6x platinum.
fun_facts: Tragic coda: Silentó was sentenced in 2025 to 30 years after pleading guilty to voluntary manslaughter in his cousin's 2021 killing.

name: The Dougie
originator: Cali Swag District (song); Dallas 'D-Town Boogie' lineage; Lil' Wil popularized regionally
originator_story: Named for 1980s rapper Doug E. Fresh's head-stroking move, the modern Dougie originated in Dallas ('D-Town Boogie'); Lil' Wil popularized it locally with 'My Dougie' (2007). A friend who'd attended college in Texas taught the future Cali Swag District members back in Inglewood. Credit dispute: Lil' Wil accused Cali Swag District of appropriating the Dallas dance without credit.
song: Teach Me How to Dougie (Cali Swag District)
timeline: 1980s Doug E. Fresh -> 2007 Lil' Wil 'My Dougie' (Dallas) -> 2009 CSD record/film -> 2010 viral, #28 Hot 100, platinum.
fun_facts: Wolf Blitzer did it at the 2010 Soul Train Awards. Member M-Bone (Montae Talbert) was shot and killed in 2011.

name: Floss
originator: Russell Horning (The Backpack Kid)
originator_story: Russell Horning ('Backpack Kid') popularized the floss, going viral after performing it during Katy Perry's 'Swish Swish' on SNL (May 2017). Fortnite added the 'Floss' emote (Dec 2017); Horning's family sued Epic Games (Dec 2018), one of a wave alongside 2 Milly (Milly Rock) and Alfonso Ribeiro (Carlton). The suits largely stalled on the difficulty of copyrighting individual dance steps.
song: (no single song; performed to Katy Perry 'Swish Swish' on SNL)
timeline: ~2016 floss emerges -> May 2017 SNL -> Dec 2017 Fortnite emote -> Dec 2018 lawsuit.
fun_facts: Horning: 'I don't really care about the money like that... I'm just glad it's in the game.'

name: Savage (Remix) dance
originator: Shayné & Zhané Stanley (The Nae Nae Twins)
originator_story: Shayné & Zhané Stanley, 'The Nae Nae Twins' (Elk Grove/Sacramento CA), choreographed a viral routine to the Megan Thee Stallion x Beyoncé 'Savage' remix (29 April 2020). Megan reposted and recreated their version. They were later recognized in the JaQuel Knight/Logitech labanotation copyright initiative (2021). (A separate remix challenge was choreographed by Cassidy Condie.)
timeline: 29 April 2020 remix (US #1) -> Nae Nae Twins choreography goes viral -> recognized in JaQuel Knight/Logitech copyright initiative July 2021 -> Aerie #AerieREAL campaign.
fun_facts: Distinct from Keara Wilson's original 'Savage' dance to the non-remix song.

name: The Woah
originator: DISPUTED — Young Deji vs 10k Cash vs DJ Dangerous vs TisaKorean (Houston/Dallas)
originator_story: Genuinely contested origin, primarily from Houston/Dallas Texas (NOT Milwaukee). Young Deji (Houston; Taylor Gang) claims he invented it; 10k Cash, TisaKorean and DJ Dangerous are also linked; DJ Dangerous posted 'receipts' arguing Young Deji conflated an earlier dance ('The Dejo') with The Woah. The FADER: 'The truth about who created The Woah is now murky and confusing.'
song: (used across many songs; no single song)
timeline: ~2018 emerges in Texas -> adopted by Lil Uzi Vert, Drake, Travis Scott (SNL 6 Oct 2018, criticized for not crediting the kids) -> ubiquitous 2018-2020.
fun_facts: The original brief's 'Milwaukee/KTumbla/414BigFrank' framing is NOT supported by available sources, which point to Texas — flagged as conflicting/unverified.

name: Buss It Challenge
originator: Erika Davila (@erikadavila120)
originator_story: TikTok user Erika Davila posted on 1 Jan 2021 urging 'Someone plz do this transition but all prettied up,' launching the glow-up transition trend (casual -> glam + twerk on the beat drop) to Erica Banks' 'Buss It' (which samples Nelly's 'Hot in Herre'). It's a transition trend, not set choreography.
timeline: Song May 2020 -> challenge 1 Jan 2021 -> 1M+ videos within days; song peaked #47 Hot 100 -> Travis Scott remix Feb 2021.

name: WAP Challenge
originator: Brian Esperon
originator_story: Brian Esperon (then 28, from Guam), a YouTube choreographer, uploaded his 'WAP' routine the same day the song dropped (7 Aug 2020). Cardi B tweeted his clip and learned it on Instagram Live, launching the #WAPChallenge (1.5B+ hashtag views). Per NBC News, Esperon said he 'had to fight for recognition' as he was rarely credited among the millions doing his dance.
song: WAP (Cardi B feat. Megan Thee Stallion)
timeline: Song released 7 Aug 2020 -> Esperon uploads routine same day -> Cardi B boosts it -> #WAPChallenge exceeds 1.5B views.
fun_facts: Esperon on Cardi learning it: 'When she learned it on her Instagram Live is when I completely lost it.' His dream: choreograph for Beyoncé.

name: Laffy Taffy dance (2021 TikTok)
originator: Camyra Franklin (dance); Fur-Quan Powell/FlyBoyFu (remix)
originator_story: The 2021 viral 'Laffy Taffy' dance used a remix by Fur-Quan Powell (FlyBoyFu) and was choreographed by Camyra Franklin, who told Jimmy Fallon she made it 'in five minutes while she was on the school bus.' Both are credited as creators of the Laffy Taffy dance craze (also featured in Vox's Explained 'Dance Crazes' episode). The original song 'Laffy Taffy' is by D4L (2005, #1 Hot 100 Jan 2006).
song: Laffy Taffy remix (Fur-Quan Powell, over D4L)
timeline: 2005-06 D4L song -> 2021 FlyBoyFu remix + Camyra Franklin choreography -> viral on TikTok -> performed uncredited by Addison Rae on The Tonight Show (26 March 2021).
fun_facts: Part of the March-April 2021 Addison Rae/Tonight Show credit controversy.

name: Addison Rae / Tonight Show cohort
originator: Multiple (largely Black) TikTok creators, uncredited on air
originator_story: On 26 March 2021 Addison Rae performed 8 viral TikTok dances on The Tonight Show without crediting the (largely Black) creators on air; credit was only added to the YouTube description after backlash. The 8: 'Do It Again,' 'Savage Love,' 'Corvette Corvette,' 'Laffy Taffy,' 'Savage,' 'Blinding Lights,' 'Up,' 'Fergalicious.' On 5 April 2021 Fallon brought several creators on (virtually) to perform their own dances.
timeline: 26 March 2021 uncredited performance -> backlash (The View's Sunny Hostin: 'Let's give credit to the black creators') -> 5 April 2021 Fallon hosts Mya Johnson & Chris Cotter, Dorien Scott, Fur-Quan Powell & Camyra Franklin, Snyder/Nale/Dahl, Keara Wilson.
fun_facts: Landmark moment in the reckoning over uncredited Black TikTok choreographers, alongside Renegade and the June 2021 #BlackTikTokStrike.

name: Crank That (Soulja Boy)
originator: Soulja Boy (DeAndre Cortez Way)
originator_story: Soulja Boy (then 16) created both the song AND the dance, including the 'Superman' move, and pioneered the 'post a dance tutorial' virality model — debuting the song atop another group's homemade clip on MySpace (25 Feb 2007), then curating fan videos. He denied the rumor that 'Superman that ho' referred to a sex act, calling it just a dance move.
timeline: Recorded March 2007 -> major-label release 2 May 2007 -> seven weeks at #1 Hot 100 (peak 15 Sept 2007) -> 6 Jan 2008 first song to sell 3M US digital copies.
fun_facts: Caused 'the biggest dance fad since the Macarena'; the instructional YouTube video passed 27M views by early 2008. Peak of the ringtone era.

name: Chicken Noodle Soup
originator: Harlem street dance; song by Young B (Bianca Bonnie) & DJ Webstar
originator_story: The dance originated in Harlem and pre-dated the song; rapper Young B (Bianca Bonnie), then 14, wrote the song to the existing Harlem dance ('allegedly choreographed by Allie Bernard'). DJ Webstar produced/performed. Bonnie: 'Chicken Noodle Soup is a dance that was already created in Harlem, and I used to make songs to these dances.'
song: Chicken Noodle Soup (DJ Webstar & Young B feat. The Voice of Harlem)
timeline: 2006 single (#45 Hot 100) -> viral via YouTube/MySpace/106 & Park -> j-hope (BTS) feat. Becky G trilingual remake 27 Sept 2019 (sparking #CNSChallenge on TikTok).
fun_facts: Secondary outlets report j-hope paid Bianca Bonnie $2.7M to sample the original (widely circulated but not independently verified). Chance the Rapper and Missy Elliott boosted a #ThankYouBianca hashtag.

name: Jerkin' (You're a Jerk / Teach Me How to Jerk)
originator: LA/Inland Empire street-dance culture; New Boyz & Audio Push
originator_story: Jerkin' was a late-2000s Los Angeles-metro/Inland Empire street-dance culture. New Boyz (Ben J and Legacy, Hesperia CA) pioneered jerk rap with 'You're a Jerk'; Audio Push followed with 'Teach Me How to Jerk.' Legacy taught himself to produce via YouTube tutorials; crews drove it from MySpace to YouTube to radio. Per Oktane of Audio Push: 'Jerking actually came from gang-banging.'
song: You're a Jerk (New Boyz); Teach Me How to Jerk (Audio Push)
timeline: Scene active from ~2008 on MySpace -> 'You're a Jerk' sold 53,000 iTunes copies its first week (2009) -> NYT praise -> revived on TikTok ~2021; New Boyz reunited for 'You're a Jerk 2' (18 Jan 2022).
fun_facts: Defined by skinny jeans, neon and high-tops — a rejection of baggy-pants style.

name: Cat Daddy
originator: Rej3ct Sam (Sam Awolope); popularized by Chris Brown
originator_story: Reject Sam Awolope ('Rej3ct Sam'), a friend of The Rej3ctz, originated the dance before the group set it to a song. Bounc3: 'Our bro Rej3ct Sam called us up like, I got this dance called the Cat Daddy.' The dance (mimicking buckling a seatbelt and rolling a wheelchair) became a craze after Chris Brown performed it on 106 & Park (Oct 2010).
timeline: 2010 song (earliest dance video 6 Aug 2010) -> Chris Brown popularizes it Oct 2010 -> Kate Upton's 2012 rendition hit ~1M views in 24 hours (briefly removed by YouTube as too risqué).
fun_facts: Rej3ctz member Mowii is a former Madonna backup dancer.

name: Hit the Quan
originator: Rich Homie Quan (moves); iLoveMemphis/Richard Colbert (song + challenge)
originator_story: The underlying moves were popularized by Rich Homie Quan in his April 2015 'Flex' video (initially just 'the Rich Homie Quan dance'). iLoveMemphis (Richard Colbert) made a $35 song built around it and created the #HitTheQuanChallenge, building the beat to match Rich Homie Quan's tempo 'so I could be able to hit his dance in it.'
timeline: April 2015 'Flex' video -> July 2015 'Hit the Quan' -> #15 Hot 100; video 12 Nov 2015 -> Ellen and The Real appearances.
fun_facts: Controversy: in July 2015 Colbert was criminally charged in connection with what Memphis police called a gang rape.

name: Juju on That Beat (TZ Anthem)
originator: Zay Hilfigerrr & Zayion McCall
originator_story: Detroit teens Zay Hilfigerrr (Tyjuan Peoples) and Zayion McCall created the song AND the 'Juju' dance simultaneously in 2014, built over Crime Mob's 2004 'Knuck If You Buck.' Hilfigerrr: 'JuJu is a dance I had created. Just like whip, just like nae nae.' It went viral after dance group Fresh the Clowns posted a #TZAnthemChallenge video.
song: Juju on That Beat (TZ Anthem) (Zay Hilfigerrr & Zayion McCall)
timeline: Created 2014 -> posted to YouTube 8 July 2016 -> viral on Musical.ly/Vine -> Atlantic signing -> official release 30 Sept 2016, #5 Hot 100.

name: The Running Man Challenge
originator: Kevin Vincent & Jeremiah 'Jerry' Hall (NJ teens); popularized by Univ. of Maryland players
originator_story: Hillside NJ high-schoolers Kevin Vincent and Jerry Hall filmed themselves doing the modern Jersey-club Running Man (Instagram, 7 Jan 2016); University of Maryland basketball players Jared Nickens and Jaylen Brantley popularized it. The dance is rooted in the Newark/Jersey-club scene, not invented by the teens or athletes.
timeline: Jan 2016 originals -> April 2016 Maryland players' versions go viral -> 'My Boo' re-entered Hot 100 at a new peak of #27 (21 May 2016) -> all four invited to Ellen.
fun_facts: The 1996 song never charted higher than #31 originally; the meme pushed it to #27 twenty years later.

name: Macarena
originator: Mia Frye (line dance in hit video); Los del Río (song)
originator_story: The dance evolved from audience interaction at Los del Río concerts; the canonical simplified line dance in the hit video was choreographed by Mia Frye for the Bayside Boys-remix video. Dispute: Songfacts commenters note similar moves circulated in the 1970s and nobody is certain who created the line dance (it may have developed in Mexico). Spanish group Fangoria's influential remix got no credit.
timeline: Written 1992 (Venezuela) -> released 1993 -> Miami radio + Bayside Boys English remix (1995) -> 14 weeks at #1 Hot 100 in 1996.
fun_facts: Performed at the 1996 Democratic National Convention; VH1 later ranked it the greatest one-hit wonder of all time.

name: The Ketchup Song (Aserejé)
originator: Las Ketchup (Muñoz sisters); no single named choreographer
originator_story: Las Ketchup — sisters Lola, Pilar and Lucía Muñoz of Córdoba, Spain — performed the hand-and-arm novelty choreography (produced by Manuel 'Queco' Ruiz). The dance spread as an easy novelty routine; no single named choreographer surfaced. The nonsensical chorus phonetically renders the first verse of the Sugarhill Gang's 'Rapper's Delight.'
timeline: Single 10 June 2002 -> #1 in 20+ European countries; #54 in the US -> 12M+ copies sold by 2014.
fun_facts: Spawned a Spanish 'Satanic message' urban legend; a Dominican TV station banned it.

name: Jerusalema Dance Challenge
originator: Fenómenos do Semba (Angolan dance troupe, led by Adilson Maíza)
originator_story: The Angolan dance troupe Fenómenos do Semba (led by Adilson Maíza) filmed themselves dancing the 'Dança da Família' while eating from plates in a Luanda backyard. The song is South African; the choreography is Angolan. A textbook uncredited-creators case: international media initially barely acknowledged the Angolan origins.
timeline: Song a local hit late 2019 -> Angolan challenge Feb 2020 (troupe's 5th anniversary) -> global spread during COVID lockdowns (police, health workers, European priests) -> 500M+ YouTube views.
fun_facts: Controversy: Warner Music drew criticism for copyright-claiming and monetizing fan and charity Jerusalema videos.

name: Supalonely
originator: Zoi Lerma (@zoifishh)
originator_story: Zoi Lerma created the arm-waving routine to BENEE's 'Supalonely,' posting ~3 days after the music video; it exploded during early lockdown, carried further by Charli D'Amelio.
timeline: Feb 2020 dance -> exploded March 2020 during early lockdown -> Hot 100 hit.

name: Out West Challenge
originator: Nicole Bloomgarden
originator_story: TikTok creator Nicole Bloomgarden created the 'Out West Challenge' to JACKBOYS & Travis Scott's 'Out West.'
song: Out West (JACKBOYS & Travis Scott feat. Young Thug)
timeline: Late Dec 2019 -> viral on TikTok Feb 2020.

name: The Box
originator: No single canonical dance creator
originator_story: 'The Box' by Roddy Ricch (2020) became a massive TikTok sound but was used mostly for comedy 'how the song was made' skits recreating its squeaky intro — not one signature choreographed dance. Various choreographers made routines; none is canonical.
timeline: Late 2019 release -> 11 weeks at #1 Hot 100 (2020) -> mostly comedy/skit TikToks rather than a single dance.
fun_facts: Documented as an artist-driven sound rather than a choreographed dance.

name: Body (#BodyChallenge)
originator: Megan Thee Stallion with choreographer JaQuel Knight
originator_story: The #BodyChallenge was self-originated by Megan Thee Stallion with choreographer JaQuel Knight — explicitly distinct from 'Savage' (choreographed by TikToker Keara Wilson). It is performed in the music video.
timeline: Nov 2020 release -> #BodyChallenge in the video -> #12 Hot 100; made Megan the first woman with three #1s on Streaming Songs in one calendar year.
fun_facts: The chorus's 'body-ody-ody-ody' derives from RuPaul's Drag Race terminology.

name: Silhouette Challenge
originator: @yoelise
originator_story: TikToker @yoelise uploaded the first red-filtered silhouette version and a two-part tutorial on 14 Jan 2021 (1.7M+ views in two weeks). Began as a body-positive trend but turned dangerous when tutorials taught users to remove the red filter to expose participants without consent.
song: Put Your Head on My Shoulder (Paul Anka) / Streets (Doja Cat) mashup
timeline: 14 Jan 2021 origin -> body-positive virality -> filter-removal controversy (Rolling Stone reported YouTube was 'profiting off creeps') -> Doja Cat homage in the 'Streets' video (March 2021).
fun_facts: Uses Snapchat's 'Vin Rouge' red filter.

name: Wellerman / ShantyTok
originator: Nathan Evans (Scottish singer-postman)
originator_story: Scottish singer-postman Nathan Evans launched #ShantyTok with his rendition of 'Wellerman,' a 19th-century NZ whaling ballad of unknown authorship (covering the arrangement popularized by Bristol group The Longest Johns). TikTok's duet feature turned his solo into layered harmonies. (A singing craze, not a dance.)
song: Wellerman (Soon May the Wellerman Come) — trad., arr. Nathan Evans
timeline: Posted 27 Dec 2020 -> #ShantyTok spreads during COVID isolation -> Evans quit his postman job, signed with Polydor, reached UK #1.
fun_facts: Included as a craze per the brief; it is a shanty/singing trend, not a dance.

name: Million Dollar Baby
originator: Tommy Richman (self-originated)
originator_story: Tommy Richman filmed himself and friends dancing to the song in the studio and posted it to TikTok — the snippet went viral before the song was even finished. He pushed back on the 'TikTok artist' label ('I'm not no fuckin TikTok artist').
timeline: Teased on TikTok 13 April 2024 -> released 26 April 2024 -> debuted and peaked at #2 Hot 100 (held off by Taylor Swift & Post Malone 'Fortnight') -> 10 weeks atop the TikTok Billboard Top 50.
fun_facts: Biggest US TikTok song of 2024.

name: Gata Only
originator: No single named amateur choreographer (artist/algorithm-driven)
originator_story: FloyyMenor & Cris MJ's 'Gata Only' spread via a TikTok dance trend that brought 'nostalgia-dancing' back; Travis Scott's August 2024 dance video boosted it in the US. No single named amateur originator is documented.
timeline: Solo version 21 Dec 2023 -> remix/video 2 Feb 2024 -> viral across Latin America -> US via TikTok, #27 Hot 100 -> #1 TikTok song globally in 2024 (15M+ videos); first Chilean song to 1B Spotify streams.

name: APT.
originator: Artist-driven (ROSÉ's finger-game choreography); no single amateur originator
originator_story: Rosé's own finger-game/clapping choreography — rooted in the Korean drinking game 'apateu'/apartment — spread on TikTok as a dance challenge; no single amateur originator is documented.
timeline: Released Oct 2024 -> MV passed 800M+ views; #1 on iTunes/Apple Music/Spotify; Korean 'all-kill' -> massive global TikTok participation.

name: Tell Ur Girlfriend
originator: @who.is.ga6y (created); @laurieluna13 (popularized)
originator_story: The dance was created by TikTok user @who.is.ga6y ('Gaby') and popularized by @laurieluna13, to Lay Bankz's 'Tell Ur Girlfriend.'
timeline: March 2024 (@laurieluna13 version 22 March 2024; tutorial 8 April 2024) -> three weeks at #1 on Billboard's TikTok Top 50 -> Bankz signed via Artist Partner Group (deliberate influencer-spend strategy).

name: TGIF / Yeah Glo!
originator: Lower-confidence: TikTok audio credited '@SGC Von' (real name unpublished)
originator_story: GloRilla's 'TGIF' (2024) spawned a dance trend credited via TikTok audio/DC tags to @SGC Von; the person's real name is not published in reputable outlets. Companion track 'Yeah Glo!' had a viral challenge but no documented single creator. Lower-confidence attribution.
timeline: Song released June 2024 -> summer 2024 dance trend (Blavity confirms ~791K clips) -> creator's real name undocumented.
fun_facts: Attribution rests on TikTok's audio/DC-credit ecosystem, not major-outlet reporting; flagged as lower-confidence.

name: Big Dawgs
originator: No single named originating choreographer (artist-driven sound)
originator_story: Hanumankind & Kalmi's 'Big Dawgs' (India, 2024) spawned many independent dance covers worldwide (including classical-fusion versions). An artist-driven viral sound rather than one canonical dance.
timeline: 2024 release -> global virality and many independent covers.

name: KPop Demon Hunters — Soda Pop / Golden
originator: Professional film choreographers (Leejung Lee, Ha Seong-jin, Jo Na-In)
originator_story: For the in-film choreography, 'Soda Pop' (Saja Boys) was worked on by Leejung Lee (The Black Label) and K-Tigers' Ha Seong-jin; 'Golden' (HUNTR/X) by Jam Republic's Jo Na-In. The viral TikTok trends recreate this professional film choreography; there is no single amateur originator.
song: Soda Pop (Saja Boys) / Golden (HUNTR/X) — KPop Demon Hunters
timeline: Film released June 2025 -> 'Golden' #1 Hot 100 (8 weeks); 'Soda Pop' #3 and 'perhaps the most viral TikTok trend' -> TikTok named 'Soda Pop' Korea's Song of the Summer 2025 (970,000+ pieces of content).

name: Espresso
originator: Jasmine 'JB' Badie (staged choreography); TikTok trend fan-driven
originator_story: Sabrina Carpenter's staged 'Espresso' choreography (Coachella 2024; Short n' Sweet tour) is credited to her longtime choreographer Jasmine 'JB' Badie. The separate TikTok 'Espresso dance' trend is fan-driven with many independent tutorials (no single dominant originator).
timeline: Coachella live debut April 2024 -> Short n' Sweet tour choreography by Badie -> fan-driven TikTok trend -> jazz rendition at the 2025 Grammys.
fun_facts: Badie has also worked with Megan Thee Stallion, Tate McRae and Normani.

name: Diet Pepsi dance
originator: Caption-credited to '@jkinghong' (J. King Hong)
originator_story: The Diet Pepsi trend choreography is credited to '@jkinghong' per the official Instagram reel caption; Addison Rae's broader album-era choreographer is Danielle Polanco. Independent choreographer Danny Wijngaarden also created a widely shared routine. Caption-based creator self-credit (reliable but not press-vetted).
timeline: 2024 release -> trend choreography credited via Instagram caption to @jkinghong.
fun_facts: Credit rests on creator self-credit captions rather than journalism.

name: Aquamarine dance
originator: Caption-credited to Danny Wijngaarden (Base Dance Studios, London)
originator_story: The Aquamarine trend routine is credited to Danny Wijngaarden of Base Dance Studios, London, via TikTok/Instagram caption. Caption-based creator self-credit.
timeline: 2024 release -> routine credited via caption to Danny Wijngaarden.
fun_facts: Caption-based creator self-credit.

name: Von Dutch
originator: No single official choreographer (Charli performed it solo)
originator_story: There is no single documented official choreographer or signature backup-dancer routine for 'Von Dutch'; on the Brat/Sweat tours Charli XCX performed it solo with no dancers. YouTube 'Von Dutch choreography' clips are independent studio covers. The genuinely viral Brat TikTok dance is 'Apple' (Kelley Heyer), not 'Von Dutch.'
timeline: 2024 release -> performed solo live; no official challenge -> independent studio covers only.
fun_facts: Documented plainly to avoid inventing a creator.

originator: No single named dance creator (trend, not a single dance)
originator_story: Charli XCX's '360' generated multiple trends rather than one choreographed dance. The most-documented named creator is Aly McCormick (GWU student), who made the viral 'I'm so Kamala' edit (July 2024). Lyrics name-check Julia Fox and Gabbriette — neither a dance creator.
timeline: Released 10 May 2024 -> multiple edits/trends; Aly McCormick's 'I'm so Kamala' edit July 2024.
fun_facts: No single named TikTok dance-creator comparable to the Apple dance.

name: Carnival (mosh-pit phenomenon)
originator: Not a choreographed dance — no single creator
originator_story: 'Carnival' by Kanye West & Ty Dolla Sign is not a choreographed dance but a stadium 'rage'/mosh-pit phenomenon driven by the song's Inter Milan ultras chant. No single creator.
song: Carnival (¥$ — Kanye West & Ty Dolla Sign feat. Rich the Kid & Playboi Carti)
timeline: Single 15 Feb 2024 -> mosh-pit phenomenon (violent brawl at Rolling Loud March 2024) -> #1 Hot 100 (West's first #1 as lead artist since 2007).
fun_facts: Documented as a concert/mosh phenomenon, not a dance.

name: Water (the Water challenge)
originator: Lee-ché 'Litchi' Janecke (choreographer)
originator_story: South African choreographer Lee-ché 'Litchi' Janecke created the routine, adapting the Pretoria-origin Bacardi dance style; the choreography was originally intended for a different song before Tyla insisted it would work with 'Water,' adding the iconic water-pour. Janecke was VMA-nominated for Best Choreography (Push 2 Start, 2025).
timeline: Snippet/challenge teased on TikTok July 2023 -> viral August 2023 -> US radio Oct 2023 -> Grammy (Best African Music Performance) and VMA (Best Afrobeats Video) 2024.
fun_facts: A named, properly-credited choreographer behind a global Afrobeats/amapiano crossover hit.

name: Tshwala Bam dance challenge
originator: Ama Quality Boys (dance group clip launched it); no single choreographer
originator_story: The challenge took off when an unknown TikTok user paired the song with a clip of the Ama Quality Boys dance group (synchronized neck/head/shoulder moves) — that clip, not an official routine, launched the craze. No single named choreographer.
song: Tshwala Bam (TitoM & Yuppe feat. S.N.E & EeQue)
timeline: Released 23 Feb 2024 -> Ama Quality Boys clip launches challenge -> Burna Boy remix 15 May 2024 -> debuted #26 Billboard US Afrobeats Songs.

name: Buga dance challenge
originator: Kizz Daniel (launched it); Poco Lee popularized the moves
originator_story: Kizz Daniel launched the challenge himself on release day with a ₦1,000,000 prize; Nigerian dancer Poco Lee is closely associated with popularizing the moves. No single named choreographer of record.
timeline: 2022 release -> Kizz Daniel launches challenge with prize -> Poco Lee popularizes the moves.
fun_facts: Controversy: a 2024 public royalties dispute between Kizz Daniel and Tekno.

name: Calm Down dance challenge
originator: Loïc Ngumele Sipeyou ('Loïc Reyel')
originator_story: Cameroon-born, Montreal-based dancer Loïc Reyel created the choreography: per The Conversation, 'On 7 March 2022 the now-famous choreography for Calm Down first appeared on the TikTok handle Loïc Reyeltv.'
song: Calm Down (Rema; remix with Selena Gomez)
timeline: Choreography posted 7 March 2022 -> global virality -> remix with Selena Gomez boosts it further.
fun_facts: Political note: in March 2023 five teenage girls in Tehran were detained and made to apologise publicly after posting the Calm Down challenge.

name: Azonto
originator: Communal Ghanaian (Ga) social dance; no single creator
originator_story: A Ghanaian (Ga) social dance descended from the traditional Kpanlogo dance, originally called 'Apaa' ('work for pay') in Accra's coastal communities. No single creator; footballer Asamoah Gyan helped popularize it via goal celebrations. Spread via amateur YouTube videos in 2011 and the diaspora.
song: associated with Sarkodie & E.L. 'U Go Kill Me' (2011) and Fuse ODG 'Azonto'
timeline: Traditional Kpanlogo roots -> craze ignites 2011 with 'U Go Kill Me' -> spreads via YouTube and the Ghanaian diaspora (Fuse ODG).

name: Skelewu dance challenge
originator: Davido (promoted via tutorial + contest); winner Bello Moshood Abiola
originator_story: No single named choreographer invented the move; Davido promoted it via an instructional dance video (18 Aug 2013) and a label dance contest, declaring fan Bello Moshood Abiola the winner on 10 Oct 2013. Modeled on Fuse ODG's 'Antenna' competition.
timeline: Instructional video 18 Aug 2013 -> HKN Music dance contest -> winner announced 10 Oct 2013 -> official video (London) Oct 2013.
fun_facts: A leaked first music video led Davido to disavow it before the official release.

name: Super Shy / #ImSuperShy challenge
originator: NewJeans (ADOR/HYBE production choreography)
originator_story: Group choreography (ADOR/HYBE production) prominently featuring waacking; NewJeans partnered with YouTube Shorts for the official #ImSuperShy dance challenge. The specific individual choreographer is not named in the sources gathered — documented as agency/production choreography.
timeline: Released 7 July 2023 -> official #ImSuperShy challenge via YouTube Shorts.

name: The Twist
originator: Hank Ballard (song); Chubby Checker (popularizer); Black social-dance roots
originator_story: Written and first recorded by Hank Ballard and the Midnighters (1958/59); popularized by Chubby Checker's 1960 cover via American Bandstand. The dance is a Black social/R&B-and-gospel-rooted form predating the record. First major 'apart' (non-partner) dance craze; reached #1 in two separate runs (1960, 1962).
timeline: 1958/59 Ballard original -> Checker's cover and TV debut 6 Aug 1960 -> #1 in 1960 and again 1962.
fun_facts: A 2014 interview claim alleges the Gospel Consolaters' Nathaniel Bills originally wrote it.

name: The Hustle
originator: South Bronx Puerto Rican teens (dance, ~1972); Van McCoy (song)
originator_story: The dance predates and inspired the song — the Latin/New York Hustle was developed by Puerto Rican teenagers in the South Bronx (~1972), evolving from a dance called 'the 500.' Van McCoy wrote the 1975 #1 hit after his partner Charles Kipps saw the dance at a discotheque (McCoy reportedly wrote it without seeing the dance himself).
song: The Hustle (Van McCoy and the Soul City Symphony)
timeline: Dance developed ~1972 in the South Bronx -> Van McCoy 'The Hustle' (1975, #1) turns the craze national.

name: Vogue / voguing
originator: Harlem Black & Latino LGBTQ ballroom scene; video choreo by House of Xtravaganza
originator_story: Voguing originated in Harlem's Black and Latino LGBTQ ballroom scene (1960s-80s), not with Madonna. Madonna's 1990 video was choreographed by Jose Gutierez Xtravaganza and Luis Camacho of the House of Xtravaganza. Widely criticized for whitewashing the originating Black/Latino queer culture (all celebrities named in the song are white).
timeline: Ballroom origins 1960s-80s (documented in Paris Is Burning, 1990) -> Madonna 'Vogue' (1990) brings voguing to the mainstream.
fun_facts: Jose Xtravaganza defends Madonna, saying she 'came to the club herself and sought us out.' Voguing's precise origins are themselves disputed.

name: Cha Cha Slide
originator: Willie Perry Jr. (DJ Casper / Mr. C the Slide Man)
originator_story: DJ Casper (Willie Perry Jr.) originally wrote 'The Cha-Cha Slide' (~1998) as a step-aerobics routine for his nephew, a fitness trainer at a Chicago Bally Total Fitness. It's both the song and the dance (call-and-response instructions).
song: Cha Cha Slide (Casper Slide Pt. 2) (Mr. C the Slide Man / DJ Casper)
timeline: 'Casper Slide Part 1' (Jan 1998) -> public release 2 Aug 2000 -> UK #1 March 2004. Perry died 7 Aug 2023, age 58.
fun_facts: A 2011 copyright lawsuit (Perry & Jerome Haywood v. M.O.B. Productions).

name: Cupid Shuffle
originator: Bryson 'Cupid' Bernard
originator_story: Bryson 'Cupid' Bernard both wrote the song and choreographed the line dance, improvising both in the studio in 2006 — a genre-neutral, lyrics-cued 32-count line dance.
timeline: 2006 studio creation -> 2007 release -> Guinness World Record (2008) for largest line dance (17,000+ in Atlanta) -> RIAA 5x Platinum (2022).

name: Electric Slide
originator: Ric Silver (claims the 22-step dance, 1976) — disputed
originator_story: Choreographer Ric Silver claims he created the 22-step Electric Slide in 1976, danced to 'Electric Boogie' (written by Bunny Wailer; recorded by Marcia Griffiths). In 2007 Silver issued DMCA takedowns against YouTube videos; the EFF sued (Machulis v. Silver) and settled, with Silver licensing the dance under Creative Commons. Legal experts note social dance steps are generally not copyrightable.
timeline: Silver's 1976 choreography -> Marcia Griffiths recording (1982; 1989 remix) -> 2007 DMCA dispute and EFF settlement.
fun_facts: The widely danced version is an 18-step variant; a notable choreography-copyright dispute.

name: The Wobble
originator: Philadelphia 'Nasty Girl' line dance (Joe 'Butterball' Tamburro & Dave Bush Jr.); V.I.C. song
originator_story: The dance's moves trace to a 1990s Philadelphia line dance, the 'Nasty Girl,' choreographed by Joe 'Butterball' Tamburro and Dave Bush Jr. ('Godfather of Line Dance'). V.I.C.'s song (15 July 2008) became the standard music; the dance surged years later via TikTok and weddings.
timeline: 1990s Philly 'Nasty Girl' line dance -> V.I.C. 'Wobble' (2008) becomes the standard music -> TikTok/wedding revival.
fun_facts: Claims that 'Jeremy Strong of Texas' renamed/originated it are contested by the Philadelphia account.

name: Stanky Legg
originator: GS Boyz (Arlington, Texas)
originator_story: The GS Boyz (snap group from Arlington, Texas) created their signature move; the dance is part of Dallas's 'D-Town Boogie' scene.
timeline: Posted to YouTube summer 2008 -> #49 Hot 100 -> used as a goal celebration by US soccer players (2009 Confederations Cup).

name: Tap In dance
originator: @yodamnmomma
originator_story: TikToker @yodamnmomma created the 'Tap In' dance (29 June 2020); popularized in July 2020 by Charli D'Amelio and Ana Coto — and D'Amelio credited @yodamnmomma. Contrary to a common conflation, there is no documented credit dispute denying the creator here (the famous uncredited-teen case is Jalaiah Harmon's 'Renegade,' a separate dance).
timeline: @yodamnmomma posts 29 June 2020 -> popularized July 2020 (with creator credit) -> song virality reportedly engineered by agency VRTCL.

name: Astronaut in the Ocean
originator: Not a dance — transition/beat-drop trend, no named creator
originator_story: No signature dance challenge or named creator: the TikTok trend was a transition/beat-drop meme (intro build-up, then action on the drop). Various independent dance covers exist but none is the origin.
timeline: Song 2019 -> viral re-release 2021 as a transition/beat-drop trend.
fun_facts: Documented as a transition meme, not a choreographed dance.

name: HOT TO GO!
originator: Chappell Roan (artist = choreographer)
originator_story: Chappell Roan personally created the arm-spelling chorus dance (H-O-T-T-O-G-O) for audience participation, built two weeks before her 2023 tour and explicitly modeled on the Village People's 'YMCA.' She told NME: 'I was really jealous of the YMCA. So I made H-O-T-T-O-G-O.' No credit dispute; she is consistently credited as creator.
timeline: Origin Aug 2023 -> charts May 2024 after 'Good Luck, Babe!' -> summer-2024 festival virality (Lollapalooza, Bonnaroo).
fun_facts: A 'queer cheer song'; the MV featured drag performers and her grandparents. Parodied on SNL season 50.

name: Wipe It Down Challenge
originator: Challenge originated by TikToker (rominagafur, contested w/ Lauren Compton); song by BMW Kenny
originator_story: The song is by BMW Kenny (Kenneth Coby, Atlanta). The mirror-wipe transition challenge was originated by a TikTok user — most cited is rominagafur (Know Your Meme dates the clip to 26 April 2020), notably set to Roddy Ricch's 'The Box,' not Coby's song; Lauren Compton also has a competing-origin claim. The song-set version scaled via a paid giveaway with Liane V.
timeline: Late April 2020 origin -> ~1.5M+ TikToks by June 2020 -> 460M+ hashtag views; Spotify Global Viral 50.
fun_facts: Rolling Stone called it 'a corporate advertising wet-dream for cleaning brands during a pandemic.' Coby later released a 'Wipe It Down' mobile game.

name: A Bar Song (Tipsy) line dance
originator: Ben Murphy (line-dance choreographer); artist Shaboozey is separate
originator_story: Germany-based line-dance choreographer Ben Murphy wrote the most widely danced 32-count, 4-wall beginner line dance (April 2024); the song's artist Shaboozey is distinct from the dance's choreographer. Competing routines exist (Aurora Profumo, Susan Garrett, Helaine Norman). Line-dance communities flag uncertainty about Murphy, so this is documented-but-low-profile.
timeline: Song hit Hot 100 top ten week of 11 May 2024 -> #1 week of 13 July 2024 (19 non-consecutive weeks, tying 'Old Town Road') -> line dance spreads mid-2024.
fun_facts: Part of a 2024 country/line-dance resurgence; exists in beginner, partner and contra versions.

name: Captain Hook Challenge
originator: Skaibeauty (viral TikTok choreography); Megan Thee Stallion launched it
originator_story: Megan Thee Stallion released 'Captain Hook' (from Suga) on 10 March 2020 and posted her own routine first on Triller, but the version that went viral on TikTok was choreographed by TikTok creator Skaibeauty.
timeline: EP Suga early March 2020 -> single + #captainhookchallenge 9-10 March 2020 -> spread across TikTok/Instagram/Triller within days.
fun_facts: Notable as an artist seeding a challenge on Triller rather than TikTok during the apps' rivalry; came amid Megan's dispute with label 1501 Certified.

name: Messy
originator: No single choreographer (quasi-dance); sparked by Sofia Richie Grainge
originator_story: Billboard called it a 'quasi-dance craze.' The song is by Lola Young; the mainstream spark is credited to Sofia Richie Grainge, whose casual finger-guns/two-step clip (~28 Nov 2024) sent weekly streams from 3.9M to 9.1M. Multiple choreographers later set formal routines, but there is no canonical originator dance.
timeline: Released May 2024 -> viral late Nov 2024 -> UK #1, 3x Platinum; Young won Best Pop Performance at the 2026 Grammys for 'Messy.'
fun_facts: Lyrically about self-sabotage; the 'Sofia Richie dance' became its own meta-trend label.

name: Zanku (Leg Work)
originator: Zlatan Ibile (artist = dance originator)
originator_story: Nigerian rapper Zlatan Ibile is consistently credited as creator of Zanku (acronym 'Zlatan Abeg No Kill Us'). It first appeared in Chinko Ekun's 'Able God' video (late 2018); Zlatan crystallized ownership with his single 'Zanku (Leg Work),' shouting out the dance's home of Agege, Lagos. Some claims tie the steps to traditional Benin/Edo practices, but these are unsubstantiated and Zlatan maintains it is his original creation.
timeline: 2018 street origin (Able God) -> 2019 nationwide craze -> 2020 international recognition via Beyoncé's 'Already' (Black Is King).
fun_facts: Signature jump-kick succeeded Shaku Shaku as Lagos's dominant street dance. Zlatan: 'I introduced Zanku and dealt the death knell to Shaku Shaku.'

name: Shaku Shaku
originator: Collective Agege/Lagos street dance (no single creator); popularized by Olamide
originator_story: No single creator — a collective street dance from Agege, Lagos (~mid-2017), with the 'awawa boys' credited by some accounts. Rapper Olamide is the popularizer who brought it to the mainstream, not the inventor, putting Shaku Shaku dancers in his 'Wo!!' video and showcasing it at OLIC 4 (Dec 2017).
timeline: Mid-2017 street -> late 2017 mainstream via Olamide/OLIC 4 -> 2018 peak.
fun_facts: Nicknamed 'the Nigerian Gangnam Style'; launched the careers of Mr. Real, Slimcase, Idowest.

name: Gwara Gwara
originator: DJ Bongz (creator); Babes Wodumo popularized
originator_story: South African DJ/producer DJ Bongz (Durban) is credited as creator, spreading the move via Instagram hashtag challenges around 2016. He told the Sunday Times: 'I created the dance from scratch.' Babes Wodumo helped popularize it. Bongz claimed to have 'trademarked' the dance, but a copyright lawyer noted you cannot trademark a dance move beyond a name/logo; no successful suit followed.
timeline: 2016 SA schoolyards -> 2018 global via Rihanna (Grammys, Jan 2018) and Childish Gambino ('This Is America,' May 2018).
fun_facts: Frequently confused with the Texan 'stanky leg' (GS Boyz, 2008); Bongz held US workshops after the Grammys.

name: Coupé-Décalé
originator: Douk Saga & the Jet Set (collective; DJ Jacob coined the dance terms)
originator_story: Ivorian performer Douk Saga (Stéphane Doucouré, 1974-2006) and his Paris-based collective the Jet Set are credited as creators/globalizers; DJ Jacob is credited with coining 'coupé'/'décalé' for the movements. A collective/movement origin with Douk Saga as central figure, developed ~2000-2002 in Afro-Parisian nightclubs.
timeline: ~2000 emergence in Paris -> 'Sagacité' (2002-03) the genre's first hit, resonating during the First Ivorian Civil War -> three documented waves through ~2010.
fun_facts: Name from Nouchi slang ('coupé' = cheat/get money, 'décalé' = run away); drew on Congolese ndombolo and Ivorian zouglou. Douk Saga died Oct 2006.

name: Kukere / Etighi
originator: Traditional Efik/Ibibio dance (no single creator); popularized by Iyanya
originator_story: The Etighi dance is a TRADITIONAL dance of the Akwa Ibom/Efik/Ibibio people of Cross River, Nigeria — no single modern creator. Singer Iyanya (himself of Efik origin) popularized it nationally with his 2012 hit 'Kukere,' made with producer D'Tunes after noticing the dance lacked a signature track.
timeline: Traditional (ancient) -> 'Kukere' Nov 2011, video May 2012 -> 2012 mainstream Nigeria/Ghana/UK diaspora; spawned MC Galaxy's 'Sekem' (2014).
fun_facts: Won Best Pop Single at The Headies 2012; 'Kukere' means 'don't worry' in Efik.

name: Zazoo Zeh
originator: Credit dispute: Portable vs Poco Lee (feat. Olamide); street legwork, no clean choreographer
originator_story: Documented credit dispute. The song is credited variously to Portable (Habeeb Okikiola) and dancer/hypeman Poco Lee, featuring Olamide. In 2021 Portable publicly accused Poco Lee of 'collecting his money and taking credit'; Portable later apologized. The dance is a street/legwork routine with no single choreographer cleanly credited.
song: ZaZoo Zehh (Poco Lee / Portable feat. Olamide)
timeline: Late 2021 origin/virality (1.7M Audiomack plays in 2 days) -> official TG Omori video Dec 2021/early 2022.
fun_facts: Portable's Instagram following jumped from ~20,000 to 500,000+ in days; 'Zazu Zeh' became everyday Nigerian slang.

name: Lambada (the Forbidden Dance)
originator: Brazilian partner dance (no single creator); song an unauthorized adaptation of Los Kjarkas
originator_story: The DANCE is a Brazilian partner dance from Pará state (maxixe/carimbó/forró roots) — no single creator. Kaoma's 1989 song 'Lambada' was an unauthorized adaptation: the melody belongs to Bolivian group Los Kjarkas (1981, 'Llorando se fue'). Kaoma credited a fictitious 'Chico de Oliveira'; Le Monde exposed it; Los Kjarkas won a 1990 lawsuit.
song: Lambada (Kaoma) — cover of 'Llorando se fue' (Los Kjarkas, 1981)
timeline: Dance is older -> Kaoma single July 1989 -> 'forbidden dance' craze, 1990 film The Forbidden Dance -> Los Kjarkas win plagiarism suit 1990.
fun_facts: Gonzalo Hermosa (Los Kjarkas) in 2025 framed it as 'cultural erasure' of Andean/Bolivian origins despite the financial settlement.

name: Magnetic (ILLIT)
originator: James / Zhao Yufan (BigHit trainee, now of CORTIS)
originator_story: The signature 'lalalala' chorus hand-point choreography for ILLIT's 'Magnetic' was created by James (Zhao Yufan), then a BigHit Music trainee and now a member of CORTIS. HYBE staff group-trained it, but James is the documented originator of the move (confirmed via Rolling Stone's CORTIS feature and documentary).
timeline: Debut EP SUPER REAL ME 25 March 2024 -> viral point-dance challenge spring/summer 2024.
fun_facts: An early viral tutorial mislabeled the choreographer; the verified creator is James/CORTIS.

name: Supernova (aespa)
originator: Bada Lee and Yeojin (per choreographer bios/official tutorial)
originator_story: Choreography credited to Bada Lee (among her credits) and Yeojin (Jeon Yeo-jin, via the official aespa 'Supernova' tutorial). The song's Wikipedia lists no choreographer; attribution comes from the choreographers' bios and official tutorial content.
timeline: From album Armageddon (SM, 13 May 2024) -> 11 non-consecutive weeks atop Circle Digital Chart, 2024's best-performing single -> dance-break drove the TikTok challenge.
fun_facts: Won Song of the Year at 2024 MAMA and Melon Music Awards.

name: Queencard ((G)I-DLE)
originator: YELL (1MILLION Dance Studio)
originator_story: Choreography credited to YELL, a choreographer affiliated with 1MILLION Dance Studio (documented on 1MILLION's official channel). Cube Entertainment is the label. Moderate-strong attribution.
timeline: EP I feel (15 May 2023) -> viral point-dance challenge summer 2023.

name: TT (TWICE)
originator: Lia Kim (1MILLION Dance Studio)
originator_story: Lia Kim (Kim Hye-rang) of 1MILLION Dance Studio created the signature 'TT' finger-to-face move (miming the crying emoticon). Strong attribution — Lia Kim described creating the move on KBS radio; corroborated by Soompi/Koreaboo. (Not Kiel Tutin, who choreographed later TWICE songs.)
timeline: EP Twicecoaster: Lane 1 (24 Oct 2016) -> one of K-pop's most imitated gestures, late 2016-2017.
fun_facts: The move mimes the 'TT' crying emoticon.

name: Butter (BTS)
originator: Son Sung-deuk (BTS in-house performance director)
originator_story: BTS's in-house performance director Son Sung-deuk (Big Hit/HYBE) is documented as central to the 'Butter' choreography. For 'Permission to Dance' (9 July 2021) no external choreographer is publicly credited (likely in-house, unconfirmed); its choreography incorporates international sign language for 'fun/dance/peace.' External names (Sienna Lalau, Nick Joseph) apply to specific later live performances, not the original MVs.
timeline: 'Butter' 21 May 2021; 'Permission to Dance' 9 July 2021 -> global virality 2021.
fun_facts: Son Sung-deuk: moderate-strong attribution; 'Permission to Dance' individual credit uncertain.

name: How You Like That (BLACKPINK)
originator: Kiel Tutin (with Kyle Hanagami, Sienna LaLau)
originator_story: Choreography credited to Kiel Tutin (British/NZ choreographer and BLACKPINK creative director), with Kyle Hanagami and Sienna LaLau per IMDb full credits. Strong attribution; Tutin publicly documented his choreography process.
timeline: Released 26 June 2020 (YG) -> viral point-dance summer 2020.
fun_facts: Other Tutin BLACKPINK credits: 'DDU-DU DDU-DU,' 'Kill This Love,' 'Pink Venom,' 'Lovesick Girls,' Jennie's 'Solo.'

name: Smart (LE SSERAFIM)
originator: No individual choreographer documented (Source Music / HYBE only)
originator_story: No individual choreographer documented in reliable sources for 'Smart' — only Source Music / HYBE. Fan/tutorial sources speculate performance director Park So-yeon is 'usually involved,' but this is unconfirmed. Report agency only.
timeline: MV 5 March 2024 (from EP EASY) -> point-dance challenge spring 2024.
fun_facts: Attribution gap: individual choreographer uncertain.

name: The Mashed Potato
originator: Rooted in African American footwork (no single inventor); James Brown popularized; Dee Dee Sharp's song made it a craze
originator_story: The move was made famous by James Brown (1959, used in his concerts) and is rooted in older African American stepping traditions — no single inventor. Dee Dee Sharp's 'Mashed Potato Time' (1962) popularized it as a craze; The Contours' 'Do You Love Me' (Berry Gordy Jr., 1962) named it internationally.
timeline: Dance ~1959 (James Brown) -> 'Mashed Potato Time' 1962 craze.
fun_facts: Under 1962 segregation, Black dancers/choreographers remained largely uncredited; Sharp asserted ownership by co-writing the sequel 'Gravy (For My Mashed Potatoes).'

name: The Loco-Motion
originator: Little Eva (Eva Boyd) invented a dance for a song written by Goffin & King
originator_story: The song was written by Gerry Goffin and Carole King (1962). The song PRE-DATED the dance: when it became a hit, singer Little Eva (Eva Boyd, King's babysitter) had to invent a dance to go with it. Carole King confirmed on NPR (2003) that the legend of Eva spontaneously dancing and inspiring the song is false.
timeline: 1962 US #1 -> #1 again for Grand Funk Railroad (1974) -> US #3 for Kylie Minogue (1988); one of few songs to top with three different acts in three decades.
fun_facts: Eva Boyd earned ~$50/week during her recording years and eventually ~$30,000 in royalties but never owned her recording rights; she died of cervical cancer in 2003 — a documented under-compensated Black artist.

name: Y.M.C.A. arm-letters
originator: Collective — emerged from the American Bandstand studio audience (6 Jan 1979)
originator_story: No single creator: the arm-letters spelling Y-M-C-A emerged spontaneously from the American Bandstand studio audience on the 6 Jan 1979 episode. The Village People did not invent it; lead singer Victor Willis adopted it after host Dick Clark replayed the audience footage.
timeline: Audience originates the letters 6 Jan 1979 -> Willis becomes first member to do it on camera -> perennial at sports events/weddings.
fun_facts: A gay anthem (McBurney YMCA inspired it); Willis (heterosexual) says he wrote it about Black urban youth. In 2015 Willis won a legal case establishing he and Morali wrote the songs without Henri Belolo.

name: The Time Warp
originator: Richard O'Brien (wrote song/routine for Rocky Horror) — stage/film routine, not a social dance
originator_story: A stage/film routine, not a single-author social dance. The song was written by Richard O'Brien (with Richard Hartley) for the 1973 stage musical The Rocky Horror Show; director Jim Sharman requested a song-and-dance number and the lyrics double as dance instructions.
timeline: 1973 stage -> 1975 film The Rocky Horror Picture Show -> staple of audience-participation midnight screenings; 50th-anniversary restored re-release 2025.
fun_facts: Sharman wanted something like the Madison line-dance scene in Godard's Bande à part. Patricia Quinn noted only the Hokey Pokey similarly instructs its own dance.

name: The Chicken Dance (Der Ententanz)
originator: Tune by Werner Thomas (1950s); dance moves have no known single choreographer
originator_story: The tune was composed by Swiss accordionist Werner Thomas in the 1950s as 'Der Ententanz' (The Duck Dance). The dance moves have no documented single choreographer — Wikipedia states their origin 'is not known'; they evolved collectively as the song spread. The 'chicken' name reportedly arose at a 1981 Tulsa Oktoberfest when a chicken costume replaced a duck costume.
timeline: Composed 1950s -> Belgian producer releases lyric versions 1970s -> global spread 1980s.
fun_facts: 140+ recorded versions, ~40M records; BBC voted it 'the most annoying song of all time' in 2000.

name: Not Like Us
originator: No single choreographed challenge — artist-driven cultural/mosh moment
originator_story: Kendrick Lamar's 'Not Like Us' became a cultural/mosh anthem rather than a choreographer-owned dance, peaking with The Pop Out: Ken & Friends concert (Juneteenth, 19 June 2024) and the Super Bowl LIX halftime show (2025). A loose 'Not Like Us Shuffle' circulated on TikTok with no documented originator (caption-only, lower-confidence).
timeline: Released May 2024 (Drake diss) -> The Pop Out concert 19 June 2024 -> Super Bowl LIX halftime Feb 2025; Grammy wins.
fun_facts: Documented as a cultural moment, not a single-creator dance.

name: Squabble Up
originator: Showcase of Oakland turfing + video choreography by Charm La'Donna; no single TikTok originator
originator_story: Kendrick Lamar's 'Squabble Up' (GNX, Nov 2024) video, directed by Calmatic, features Oakland turf dancers and choreographed numbers by Charm La'Donna. Turfing is a Bay Area street dance form; this is a showcase of regional Black dance, not a TikTok challenge with a single originator. It went viral via a Mercedes-AMG F1 promo and an NBA broadcast.
timeline: Released 22/26 Nov 2024 -> viral via F1 promo and NBA broadcast -> samples Debbie Deb's 1984 'When I Hear Music.'
fun_facts: Foregrounds Bay Area turfing dancers (Icecold 3000, Velo, Dmonte, Aktive); choreography by Charm La'Donna.

name: Type Shit
originator: No single creator — lip-sync/audio trend
originator_story: 'Type Shit' (Future, Metro Boomin, Travis Scott, Playboi Carti, 2024) had no documented dedicated dance challenge with a named originator; it was primarily a lip-sync/audio trend.
song: Type Shit (Future, Metro Boomin, Travis Scott & Playboi Carti)
timeline: Released March 2024 (We Don't Trust You era) -> audio/lip-sync trend.
fun_facts: Documented as an audio trend, not a choreographed dance.

name: Bad Bunny — Debí Tirar Más Fotos / PR residency
originator: Afro-Puerto Rican folk forms (bomba, plena, jíbaro) — no single creator
originator_story: Bad Bunny's 'Debí Tirar Más Fotos' (Jan 2025) and his 2025 Puerto Rico residency foregrounded bomba, plena and jíbaro traditions — centuries-old Afro-Puerto Rican folk forms (bomba traces to enslaved Africans brought to PR in the 1500s), not single-creator challenges. Opening night percussion was led by Julito Gastón; the album features Los Pleneros de la Cresta.
timeline: Album 5 Jan 2025 -> 'No Me Quiero Ir de Aquí' residency 11 July-20 Sept 2025 (31 dates, Coliseo de Puerto Rico).
fun_facts: Framed as a 'love letter' to Puerto Rico spotlighting traditional Afro-Puerto Rican forms; not a single-creator dance.

name: Push 2 Start
originator: Lee-ché Janecke ('Litchi Hov') — choreographer
originator_story: South African choreographer Lee-ché Janecke ('Litchi Hov'), also behind Tyla's 'Water' challenge, choreographed 'Push 2 Start.' Distinguish: Tyla is the recording artist; Janecke is the choreographer/originator. The video was directed by Aerin Moreno with creative director Thato Nzimande.
timeline: MV premiered 8 Nov 2024 -> Best Choreography AND Best Afrobeats nominations at the 2025 MTV VMAs (7 Sept 2025); Tyla won Best Afrobeats.
fun_facts: A clearly-credited choreographer behind a global Afrobeats challenge — notable given how often African dance forms travel uncredited.

name: Mnike
originator: Rozette (@ro.makka) — dance originator
originator_story: The viral TikTok dance to 'Mnike' was created by Rozette (@ro.makka), then a 17-year-old from Langebaan, Western Cape, who posted her choreography around April 2023. She told BONA Magazine: 'The moment I heard the Amapiano song, I started to make my own moves.' Distinguish: the song artists vs. the dance originator Rozette.
song: Mnike (Tyler ICU & Tumelo.za feat. DJ Maphorisa, Nandipha808, Ceeka RSA & Tyron Dee)
timeline: Song 2023 -> Rozette's choreography ~April 2023 -> global amapiano dance challenge.
fun_facts: A clearly-credited South African teen originator.

name: Rush
originator: Distributed/organic afrodance challenge — no single definitive originator
originator_story: Ayra Starr's 'Rush' (2022, Mavin) was a major Afrobeats/afrodance challenge with no single definitive originator established in reliable sources; multiple dancers posted widely-copied versions. Lower-confidence on any single creator.
timeline: Released 2022 -> organic afrodance challenge across TikTok/Instagram.
fun_facts: Documented as a distributed/organic challenge rather than single-creator.

name: Ndombolo
originator: Contested origin (Radja Kula vs Wenge Musica); popularized by Koffi Olomidé et al.
originator_story: Congolese genre/dance from the late 1990s. Origin contested: some credit dancer/choreographer Radja Kula (1995), others Wenge Musica's late-1990s influence; popularized by Koffi Olomidé ('King of Ndombolo'), Awilo Longomba, JB Mpiana, Werrason. Hip-and-waist gyrations. In Feb 2005 the DR Congo censored ndombolo videos as indecent.
song: (Soukous/ndombolo tracks by Koffi Olomidé, Awilo Longomba, etc.)
timeline: Late 1990s emergence -> mainstream via Koffi Olomidé/Awilo Longomba -> 2005 DRC broadcast censorship.
fun_facts: Ban attempts also occurred in Cameroon, Mali and Kenya.

name: Pantsula
originator: Collective township street dance (1950s-60s Johannesburg) — no single creator
originator_story: South African township street dance originating in 1950s-60s Sophiatown and Alexandra during apartheid; name means 'waddle like a duck.' Evolved from Sotho dances with 1930s American jazz influence, later absorbing hip-hop/breakdance. A collective folk/street form. Inspired the opening of Beyoncé's 'Run the World (Girls)' (2011) via the Tofo Tofo Dance Group.
timeline: 1950s-60s township origin -> kwaito-era prominence 1990s -> international visibility via Beyoncé (2011).
fun_facts: A township subculture and protest/identity dance, not single-creator.

name: Gasolina / Perreo
originator: Perreo is a Puerto Rican street/club form — no single creator
originator_story: Daddy Yankee's 'Gasolina' (2004) globalized reggaeton; the associated dance is perreo (sandungueo), rooted in Puerto Rican/Panamanian underground (late 1980s) and influenced by Jamaican dancehall, salsa and merengue. No single choreographer — a social/club dance born in Black working-class neighborhoods.
timeline: Released 2004 -> global reggaeton breakout -> inducted into the Library of Congress National Recording Registry 12 April 2023 (first reggaeton recording in the Registry).
fun_facts: Perreo is a street/club form, not a single-creator dance.

name: Danza Kuduro
originator: Kuduro is an Angolan electronic dance form — no single creator; pop hit adapted by Lucenzo/Don Omar
originator_story: 'Danza Kuduro' (Don Omar & Lucenzo, 2010) adapted Lucenzo's 'Vem Dançar Kuduro' (2010). Kuduro is an Angolan electronic dance genre/dance from the late 1980s, fusing semba with techno/house/soca. No single choreographer; the Angolan origin is frequently uncredited in coverage of the pop hit.
timeline: Kuduro late-1980s Angola -> Lucenzo 'Vem Dançar Kuduro' 2010 -> Don Omar adds Spanish lyrics/star power, global hit.
fun_facts: Artist-adapted from an Angolan folk/street form.

name: Waka Waka (This Time for Africa)
originator: Chorus borrowed from Golden Sounds/Zangalewa (Cameroon); settled out of court
originator_story: Shakira's 2010 World Cup anthem borrows its chorus and 'waka waka' hook from 'Zangaléwa' (1986) by Cameroonian makossa band Golden Sounds (later Zangalewa). After plagiarism allegations, an out-of-court Sony settlement was announced 11 May 2010 crediting Golden Sounds. A clear documented case of uncredited African originators.
song: Waka Waka (This Time for Africa) (Shakira feat. Freshlyground)
timeline: 'Zangaléwa' (1986, Cameroon) -> Shakira's 2010 World Cup version -> plagiarism allegations -> Sony settlement May 2010 crediting Golden Sounds.
fun_facts: Reported ~$400,000 advance (secondhand; terms not officially disclosed). Contrast with PSY's licensed 'Gentleman' borrowing.

name: The Charleston
originator: Song by James P. Johnson (1923); dance from Gullah/Geechie traditions
originator_story: The song 'The Charleston' was written by Black composer/pianist James P. Johnson (lyrics by Cecil Mack) for the 1923 all-Black Broadway show Runnin' Wild. The dance derives from Gullah/Geechie traditions Johnson saw among South Carolina dockworkers, with roots in the African-derived Juba dance. Popularized to white audiences by Josephine Baker and others.
timeline: Gullah/Geechie roots -> Runnin' Wild (Oct 1923) -> 1920s craze; Baker takes it to Paris 1925.
fun_facts: A documented case of Black folk origins commercialized for white audiences.

name: Lindy Hop
originator: 'Shorty George' Snowden (named it); Frankie Manning (first aerial) — Harlem Savoy Ballroom
originator_story: Harlem vernacular jazz dance (late 1920s-30s, Savoy Ballroom). 'Shorty George' Snowden is credited with naming the dance (after Lindbergh's flight); Frankie Manning pioneered the first aerial/air step (1935) and became its global ambassador, winning a 1989 Tony for choreographing Black and Blue.
timeline: Snowden reinvents the breakaway 1928 -> Manning's first aerial 1935 -> 1930s-40s peak -> revived from the 1980s.
fun_facts: Other founders: Herbert 'Whitey' White, Norma Miller, 'Twistmouth George' Ganaway. A Black American vernacular jazz dance.

name: Moving Like Bernie / The Bernie
originator: Anthony LaVarry (ISA)
originator_story: 'Moving Like Berney' (ISA, 2010) was created by Anthony LaVarry, who records as ISA, of Shreveport, Louisiana. He told The New Republic the song began when his daughter pushed him back into the studio and he freestyled about Weekend at Bernie's; he credited his little brother with the moves. The wobble imitates the reanimated corpse from Weekend at Bernie's II (1993).
timeline: Released 2010 -> viral 2010-2012 -> adopted by the Oakland Athletics in 2012.
fun_facts: ISA glossed his name as both 'I'm Still Anthony' and 'Infinity So Awesome.'

name: Gentleman (PSY)
originator: 'Arrogant dance' licensed from Brown Eyed Girls' 'Abracadabra' (creators Yama & Hot Chicks)
originator_story: The central 'arrogant dance' in PSY's 'Gentleman' (2013) is borrowed from Brown Eyed Girls' 'Abracadabra' (2009); PSY's team paid copyright fees to the dance's creators 'Yama & Hot Chicks,' making it a licensed cover. Choreographer Lee Joo-sun (also behind the 'Gangnam Style' horse dance) selected it from ~50 options; G-Dragon reportedly suggested it.
timeline: 'Abracadabra' 2009 -> PSY licenses the move for 'Gentleman' April 2013.
fun_facts: A documented, properly-credited borrowing — instructive contrast with the uncredited Waka Waka case. Brown Eyed Girls' Ga-in appears in the MV.

name: Whiplash (aespa)
originator: Vessi (chorus, self-credited); 'It Girl' voguing concept by Harimu & Vessi
originator_story: aespa's 'Whiplash' (Oct 2024, SM) chorus/hook choreography is self-credited to Vessi; the voguing concept is credited to Harimu & Vessi, with the La Chica crew also involved. SM did not publish a single official individual credit (liner notes omit a choreographer). Medium-high confidence for Vessi (chorus).
timeline: Released 21 Oct 2024 -> viral point-dance challenge late 2024-2025.
fun_facts: Agency liner notes omit a choreographer; attribution from choreographer self-credit.

name: Hype Boy / OMG (NewJeans)
originator: Kim Eun-ju (ADOR performance director) with BLACK.Q
originator_story: NewJeans' 'Hype Boy' (Aug 2022) and 'OMG' (Jan 2023) choreography is attributed to ADOR performance director Kim Eun-ju in collaboration with BLACK.Q; CEO Min Hee-jin requested a full revision of the 'Hype Boy' choreography. High confidence for Hype Boy/OMG; attribution is interview/secondary-sourced (liner notes do not name choreographers).
timeline: 'Hype Boy' 1 Aug 2022 -> 'OMG' 2 Jan 2023 -> major member-by-member challenge formats.
fun_facts: Liner notes do not credit choreographers; attribution is interview-sourced.

name: Love Dive / I AM (IVE)
originator: Chae Dasom (Freemind) with crews La Chica & Freemind
originator_story: IVE's 'Love Dive' (Apr 2022) was choreographed by Chae Dasom (Freemind) with crews La Chica & Freemind (interview-sourced, high confidence); 'I AM' (Apr 2023) is credited to the La Chica crew (medium confidence). Starship Entertainment.
timeline: 'Love Dive' 5 April 2022 -> 'I AM' 10 April 2023 -> viral point-dance challenges.

name: Standing Next to You (Jungkook)
originator: Keone Madrid (lead choreographer)
originator_story: Jungkook's 'Standing Next to You' (Nov 2023, BIGHIT/HYBE + Geffen) was led by choreographer Keone Madrid (Filipino-American), with contributions from Shay Latukolan and Brian Puspos. High confidence (Keone Madrid self-credited; corroborated by Bandwagon and Philstar).
timeline: Released 3 Nov 2023 (Golden) -> retro funk-pop choreography and challenge.

name: God's Menu (Stray Kids)
originator: Lee Dabin & Lee Junwoo (dance team LOOK)
