originator_story: Stray Kids' 'God's Menu' (Jun 2020, JYP) was choreographed by Lee Dabin and Lee Junwoo of dance team LOOK (interview-sourced; high confidence). 'S-Class' (2023) was a team effort (performance directors Sim Kyujin & Rimmo; MOTF, Kasper, Keone Madrid per fan-documentation; medium confidence).
timeline: 'God's Menu' 17 June 2020 -> 'S-Class' 2 June 2023 -> powerful performance-driven challenges.

name: Hit or Miss / Mia Khalifa
originator: @nyannyancosplay (viral catalyst); no dance choreographer — lip-sync meme; song by iLOVEFRiDAY
originator_story: Song by Atlanta duo iLOVEFRiDAY (Aqsa Malik and Carrington Hyatt), self-released 12 Feb 2018. No designed choreography: on 10 Sept 2018, cosplayer @nyannyancosplay posted a seated lip-dub while cosplaying as Nico Yazawa from Love Live!, captioned 'Hit or miss? 😎💕' — that clip became the meme's viral catalyst.
timeline: Song 12 Feb 2018 -> @nyannyancosplay clip 10 Sept 2018 -> 1.3M TikToks by late Oct 2018, 4M+ by Feb 2019 -> Columbia re-release Dec 14 2018.
fun_facts: iLOVEFRiDAY reportedly did not receive compensation from TikTok; estimated ~$150,000 YouTube royalties per Pitchfork writer. @nyannyancosplay faced significant harassment over her viral fame.

name: Mooo! / Bitch I'm a Cow
originator: Doja Cat (artist = originator; self-created #MOOchallenge)
originator_story: No external choreographer. On 7 Aug 2018 Doja Cat improvised the song on Instagram Live in a cow-print costume, then made it and a DIY green-screen video in ~12.5 hours. She launched the official #MOOchallenge 13 Aug 2018. Both the song and the challenge are entirely her own creation.
timeline: 7 Aug 2018 Instagram Live -> 10 Aug 2018 video upload -> viral within a weekend -> #MOOchallenge 13 Aug -> 500M+ lifetime views.
fun_facts: Doja considered it a 'throwaway' joke; widely credited with setting the tone for her career. Samples Wes Montgomery's 'Polka Dots and Moonbeams.'

name: Vibe (If I Back It Up)
originator: Cookiee Kawaii (artist); no single named dance choreographer — organic TikTok trend
originator_story: Jersey club song by Cookiee Kawaii (Vanice Brittany Palmer), Irvington NJ, uploaded to SoundCloud 3 March 2019. Went viral on TikTok early 2020 via an unidentified small creator, then amplified by @treclements. No single named choreographer; Cookiee fought to be recognized as the song's creator as the trend spiralled beyond her. The FADER: Jersey club 'stood out' precisely because there was nothing else like it on TikTok.
song: Vibe (If I Back It Up) (Cookiee Kawaii)
timeline: Mar 2019 SoundCloud -> Jan 31 2020 earliest known TikTok -> Feb 2020 viral (720K+ videos, later 1.9M+) -> signed to EMPIRE March 2020 -> Tyga remix Aug 2020.
fun_facts: Cookiee: 'If you put Jersey club music on TikTok it's just gonna stand out because there's nothing else like it.'

name: M to the B (Bella Poarch)
originator: Millie B (song); Bella Poarch (viral clip) — lip-sync, not a dance
originator_story: Song 'Soph Aspin Send' ('M to the B') by Millie Bracewell (Millie B), Blackpool UK grime, released 4 Dec 2016 as a diss track at age 16. No designed dance: on 17 Aug 2020, Bella Poarch posted a lip-sync using the Face Zoom filter that became the most-liked TikTok ever. Millie B was largely uncredited in the viral wave.
song: Soph Aspin Send (M to the B) (Millie B)
timeline: Dec 2016 UK release -> 17 Aug 2020 Poarch clip -> most-liked TikTok ever (54.9M likes per Guinness World Records Jan 2022; 800M+ views by May 2024) -> revival 2022.
fun_facts: A documented under-credited creator case: Millie B's Blackpool grime track drove the most-liked TikTok of all time while she was largely ignored.

name: Roxanne challenge
originator: Arizona Zervas (artist); no single named dance originator — organic challenge
originator_story: Arizona Zervas released 'Roxanne' 10 Oct 2019 (independent, then Columbia from 15 Nov 2019). The Roxanne challenge emerged organically from the user base; no credited choreographer. Over 2.3M TikToks using the song.
timeline: 10 Oct 2019 release -> first fully independent unsigned artist to top US Spotify (weeks after release) -> No. 4 Hot 100 peak -> signed Columbia 15 Nov 2019 -> 1B+ Spotify streams by end of 2020.

name: Numa Numa Dance
originator: Gary Brolsma (originator of meme video); O-Zone (song artists)
originator_story: Gary Brolsma (NJ) uploaded 'Numa Numa Dance' to Newgrounds 6 Dec 2004 — a seated webcam lip-sync with expressive gestures to O-Zone's 'Dragostea Din Tei.' No choreography; Brolsma is the clear sole originator of the meme video.
timeline: 6 Dec 2004 Newgrounds upload -> 700M estimated views by Nov 2006 (second most-watched viral video at the time, per BBC/The Viral Factory) -> GMA / Tonight Show / VH1 appearances -> 10-Year Reunion video 2014 -> Fortnite emote 2022.
fun_facts: NYT called Brolsma 'an unwilling and embarrassed Web celebrity' (Feb 2005).

name: Caramelldansen
originator: Multi-author internet artifact (DJ Speedycake remix; '4chan Sven' Flash loop); no single human dance originator
originator_story: Song by Swedish group Caramell (album Supergott, 16 Nov 2001). The meme's sped-up audio was made by 'DJ Speedycake' (~2006); the viral 15-frame Flash loop was assembled by a 4chan user ('Sven from Sweden') using a GIF of characters from Japanese visual novel Popotan. No human dance originator — it is an animated loop (hands-on-head bunny ears, hip-sway).
timeline: 2001 release -> ~2006 Speedycake remix + Popotan Flash loop -> late 2007/early 2008 'Uma Uma Boom' on Nico Nico Douga -> global YouTube spread -> Japan Gold Disc Award (2009) -> TikTok revival ~March 2020.
fun_facts: YouTuber jan Misali documented (Oct 2021) that rights-holder Remixed Records retroactively rebranded the song to obscure Caramell and DJ Speedycake; his video was removed after copyright strikes.

name: Tunak Tunak Tun
originator: Daler Mehndi (artist = originator; self-performed video response to critics)
originator_story: Song by Punjabi Bhangra-pop artist Daler Mehndi (1998), made as a direct response to critics who said his videos were only popular because of beautiful female dancers: Mehndi responded with a video of only himself (four green-screened clones). First Indian music video made using chroma key. The 'dance' is Mehndi's own movements; the meme is built on imitation/parody.
timeline: 1998 India release -> international cult following by 1999 -> first parody by Dan(nel) Gomiller (2001, ~17,000 hits/day at peak) -> WoW Draenei dance rendition (2007) -> caption/video meme resurgence ~2017-18.
fun_facts: Title is onomatopoeia for the tumbi instrument; has no literal meaning.

name: Pedro (raccoon dance)
originator: @fleksa30 (raccoon clip originator); no human dance choreographer — animal/beat-drop meme
originator_story: Song 'Pedro' by Italian singer Raffaella Carrà (1980); viral 2024 techno remix by German producers Jaxomy and Agatino Romero (released 20 Feb 2024, after approval from Carrà's heirs). The viral raccoon clip reportedly came from Russian TikToker @fleksa30, featuring his pet raccoon 'Ginger' (renamed 'Pedro' by the internet). No human dance originator.
song: Pedro (Jaxomy & Agatino Romero remix of Raffaella Carrà, 1980)
timeline: 1980 original -> 20 Feb 2024 remix -> viral April 2024 via raccoon clip -> No. 8 US Billboard Dance/Electronic; 300K+ sold Germany.
fun_facts: Carrà died 2021; the remix introduced her to Gen Z/Millennial audiences 44 years later.

name: Makeba (Jain) dance trend
originator: No single credited choreographer; popularized organically on TikTok (~@sierrakai.1, May 2023)
originator_story: Song by French singer Jain (released 6 Nov 2015), honouring South African activist-singer Miriam Makeba. Surged on TikTok May-June 2023; @sierrakai.1 (28 May 2023) posted an 'original dance' widely copied, though Know Your Meme notes the exact first viral use is unclear. No single credited choreographer.
timeline: 2015 release -> 2016 official MV (Grammy nomination Best Music Video 2018) -> TikTok surge May-June 2023 -> No. 1 Global Shazam chart.
fun_facts: Named TikTok's 'song of the summer' 2023.

name: Naatu Naatu
originator: Prem Rakshith (choreographer) — distinct from composer M.M. Keeravani and film stars
originator_story: Telugu cinema choreographer Prem Rakshith (born Thomas Satish) created the Naatu Naatu hook step for RRR (2022, dir. S.S. Rajamouli), exploring 110+ movements before narrowing to four core moves. The suspenders prop was an on-set improvisation. S.S. Rajamouli to Vanity Fair: 'He has come up with, you wouldn't believe it, more than a hundred variations for this 3/4 signature.' Rakshith attended the Oscars and took a selfie with Rihanna.
song: Naatu Naatu (composed by M.M. Keeravani; vocals Rahul Sipligunj & Kaala Bhairava)
timeline: RRR March 2022 -> global TikTok/Instagram dance challenge -> Golden Globe Best Original Song Jan 2023 (first Asian/Indian song) -> Academy Award Best Original Song March 2023 (first from an Indian film).
fun_facts: Shot over 15 days with 150 dancers at the Ukrainian Presidential Palace in Kyiv. The Oscars live performance used US-based dancers because the Indian team didn't receive visas in time.

name: Jai Ho (Slumdog Millionaire)
originator: Longinus 'Longie' Fernandes (choreographer) — distinct from composer A.R. Rahman
originator_story: Longinus Fernandes choreographed the end-credits Jai Ho dance sequence for Slumdog Millionaire (2008), shot at Mumbai's CST station. Director Danny Boyle thanked him in his Oscar acceptance speech and apologised for missing his name in the film credits.
song: Jai Ho (composed by A.R. Rahman; vocals Sukhwinder Singh, Tanvi Shah, Mahalakshmi Iyer)
timeline: 2008 film -> Academy Award Best Original Song + Grammy Best Song Written for Visual Media (2009) -> Fernandes won the Fred Astaire Award (NY) for best film choreography.
fun_facts: The song was originally composed for Subhash Ghai's Yuvvraaj but rejected as 'too subtle'; Rahman repurposed it.

name: Kala Chashma
originator: Bosco-Caesar (Bosco Martis & Caesar Gonsalves) — film choreographers; 2022 viral popularizer: The Quick Style
originator_story: Bosco-Caesar choreographed the on-screen dance for Baar Baar Dekho (2016, Katrina Kaif/Sidharth Malhotra), documented via their 2017 Zee Cine Award for Best Choreography. The song is a recreation by Badshah of Amar Arshi's 1990 Punjabi original. Dance went massively viral globally mid-2022 when Norwegian crew The Quick Style performed it at a wedding (a reel by dancer Yasin Tatby garnered 8.5M+ Instagram views in five days).
song: Kala Chashma (vocals Amar Arshi, Badshah, Neha Kakkar)
timeline: 2016 release -> 2022 The Quick Style wedding clip -> 276K+ TikToks by mid-Aug 2022 -> Tonight Show segment.
fun_facts: Katrina Kaif commented on The Quick Style video: 'Love this – u guys are outstanding.'

name: The Madison
originator: William 'Bubbles' Holloway and African American dancers at the LVA Club, Columbus OH (~1957); no single song-artist
originator_story: The Madison is one of the first true line dances. Attributed to African American dancers in Columbus, Ohio — specifically William 'Bubbles' Holloway and the LVA Club (~late 1957). Per Columbus accounts, Holloway named it after asking how to get to Madison Avenue from Birdland in NYC and being told 'Take it to the left.' A Black-American creation.
song: The Madison (Al Brown's Tunetoppers, 1960; Madison Time, Ray Bryant Combo, 1959)
timeline: ~1957 LVA Club Columbus -> spread to Baltimore -> national exposure via The Buddy Deane Show 1960 -> craze peaked 1960-64 -> featured in Hairspray (1988 film and stage musical).
fun_facts: Called steps include the Double Cross, Cleveland Box, Jackie Gleason, Birdland, and the Rifleman. Still hugely popular in Cambodia; Guinness record of 4,999 participants in Siem Reap 15 April 2023.

name: The Lambeth Walk
originator: Lupino Lane (stage originator); Adele England (participation-dance elaborator)
originator_story: Lupino Lane popularized the jaunty Cockney strut in the 1937 musical Me and My Girl. Dance-hall instructor Adele England (Locarno Dance Halls) elaborated it into a participation dance after C.L. Heimann commissioned her to turn the walk into a full routine.
song: The Lambeth Walk (Noel Gay / Lupino Lane, from Me and My Girl, 1937)
timeline: 1937 stage (opened Dec 1937, Victoria Palace) -> 1938 international craze. The Times Oct 1938: 'While dictators rage and statesmen talk, all Europe dances to The Lambeth Walk.'
fun_facts: SA Mann denounced it Jan 1939 as 'Jewish mischief and animalistic hopping.' In 1942 Charles Ridley made a propaganda film editing Nazi goose-stepping to the tune.

name: Limbo / Limbo Rock
originator: Trinidadian folk dance (funerary origins); popularized by Julia Edwards; commercialized by Chubby Checker
originator_story: The limbo is a traditional Trinidadian (Tobago) folk dance with funerary roots, popularized in the 1950s by Trinidadian dancer Julia Edwards ('First Lady of Limbo'), who added standard features (human bars, fire). Chubby Checker, a Black American artist, commercialized it in the US via 'Limbo Rock' (1962) without originating the dance. Song written by Kal Mann (as Jan Sheldon) and Billy Strange.
timeline: 19th-century Tobago wakes -> 1950s Julia Edwards popularizes globally (films incl. Fire Down Below, 1957) -> 1962 Checker 'Limbo Rock' (#2 Billboard Hot 100) -> featured in It's a Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad World (1963).
fun_facts: The limbo is the unofficial national dance of Trinidad and Tobago. Songwriter Billy Strange wrote the tune on a $100 bet.

name: Saturday Night (Whigfield)
originator: No definitively named choreographer (folk/resort origin likely); backing dancers performed it on Top of the Pops
originator_story: No definitively named individual choreographer. Wikipedia notes 'some suggest' the routine began as an aerobic routine by a gym instructor at a holiday resort and followed the song back to the UK. The five-motion routine was performed by backing dancers on Whigfield's Top of the Pops appearance (15 Sept 1994). Song by Danish singer Whigfield (Sannie Charlotte Carlson), written by Italian producers Larry Pignagnoli and Davide Riva.
timeline: 1992 Italy release -> No. 1 Spain late 1993 -> summer 1994 British holiday-resort hit -> UK No. 1 on release (5 Sept 1994), first artist to debut at #1 with a debut single -> Top of the Pops dance 15 Sept 1994.
fun_facts: Choreographer is genuinely undocumented; 'gym instructor at a holiday resort' account is hedged in sources.

name: Koi Dance (Gen Hoshino)
originator: Mikiko Mizuno (MIKIKO) — choreographer; distinct from artist Gen Hoshino
originator_story: Mikiko Mizuno (MIKIKO), known for choreographing Perfume and Babymetal, choreographed the end-credits 'Koi Dance' for the 2016 TBS drama The Full-Time Wife Escapist (Nigeru wa Haji da ga Yaku ni Tatsu), performed by the cast. Director Kazuaki Seki directed the MV.

celebrity_performances.performer: US Ambassador Caroline Kennedy & embassy staff

timeline: 5 Oct 2016 drama/song -> cast performed the dance at the end of every episode -> nationwide craze in Japan -> Hoshino's first No. 1 on Billboard Japan Hot 100 (11 non-consecutive weeks); MV 280M+ YouTube views.
fun_facts: Leads Yui Aragaki and Gen Hoshino later married in real life (announced May 2021).

name: Old Town Road / #YeeHawChallenge
originator: No single dance creator — transformation meme seeded by Lil Nas X
originator_story: Lil Nas X deliberately uploaded 'Old Town Road' to TikTok in Feb 2019 and seeded it as a meme. The #YeeHawChallenge was a transformation meme (everyday look → cowboy), not a choreographed routine. No single dance originator.
song: Old Town Road (Lil Nas X; Billy Ray Cyrus remix Apr 5, 2019)
timeline: Dec 2018 upload -> Feb 2019 TikTok seeding -> #YeeHawChallenge viral spring 2019 -> Billboard Hot 100 #1 for record 19 consecutive weeks (Apr 13-Aug 17, 2019).
fun_facts: Tied to the 'Yeehaw Agenda' reclaiming Black cowboy culture; controversially removed from Billboard's Hot Country chart.

name: Industry Baby
originator: Sean Bankhead (music video choreographer) — NOT Tanisha Scott
originator_story: The militant prison-yard choreography in the 'Industry Baby' video was created by Sean Bankhead (per video credits and Wikipedia), homaging Janet Jackson's 'Rhythm Nation' and Michael Jackson's 'HIStory.' The broader TikTok trend was tutorial/cover-driven with no single canonical originator. NOTE: Tanisha Scott is a different Lil Nas X choreographer (Old Town Road video, Doritos Super Bowl spot).
song: Industry Baby (Lil Nas X feat. Jack Harlow)
timeline: Released 23 July 2021 -> video tutorial trend -> GRAMMY performance 2022.
fun_facts: Common mis-attribution: Tanisha Scott choreographed other Lil Nas X work, not Industry Baby.

name: Beggin' (Måneskin)
originator: No single dance creator — lip-sync/rock audio trend
originator_story: Måneskin's 2017 cover of The Four Seasons' 1967 'Beggin'' went viral on TikTok after their May 2021 Eurovision win, soundtracking 10M+ TikTok videos. A lip-sync and energy trend, not a choreographed dance. No single originator.
timeline: 2017 cover -> viral summer 2021 post-Eurovision -> No. 1 Spotify Global (July 3, 2021); #Maneskin hashtag 3.7B TikTok views.

name: Lalala
originator: No single dance creator — audio virality via engineered guerrilla marketing
originator_story: 'Lalala' (Y2K & bbno$, June 7, 2019) went viral via a guerrilla campaign (Tinder Plus, Craigslist, call centers) documented by Rolling Stone. No single dance creator; a hand-gesture trend (#lalalala_challenge) emerged in 2020 but has no documented originator.
timeline: June 7 2019 release -> Spotify Viral 50 #1 via engineered campaign -> 2020 hand-gesture resurgence.

name: Please Please Please (Sabrina Carpenter)
originator: No single named creator — multiple competing caption-only credits
originator_story: 'Please Please Please' (Sabrina Carpenter, June 2024) spawned multiple couples/'straight-face' routines by competing TikTokers (e.g., Cost n' Mayor, brookeandjess), all marked 'DC:' — caption-only, lower-confidence. No single verified originator. Later became a Fortnite emote.
timeline: June 2024 release -> competing couples trends on TikTok -> Fortnite emote.

name: Good Luck, Babe! (Chappell Roan)
originator: No single dance creator — audio trend; HOT TO GO arm-spelling dance (already cataloged) is separate
originator_story: 'Good Luck, Babe!' (Chappell Roan, April 2024) went viral as an audio trend; the notable dances associated with Roan are the HOT TO GO! arm-spelling routine (already cataloged). At Outside Lands (Aug 11, 2024) Roan called out the VIP section for not dancing — that itself became a TikTok trend. No single dance originator for 'Good Luck, Babe!' specifically.
timeline: April 2024 release -> audio virality -> notable Outside Lands moment Aug 2024.

name: Luther (Kendrick Lamar feat. SZA)
originator: No single verified originator — slow couples trend with caption-only credits
originator_story: 'Luther' (Kendrick Lamar feat. SZA, from GNX, Nov 2024) spawned a slow couples/'strike-a-pose' trend. Multiple tutorial creators (e.g., @dylansmovin, @pokemonmasterzo) hold caption-only 'DC:' credits — lower confidence. No single verified originator.
timeline: Nov 2024 (GNX) -> slow-dance/couples trend on TikTok.

name: SkeeYee (Sexyy Red)
originator: No single named dance creator in primary journalism — hair-whipping move tied to the 'skee-yee' ad-lib
originator_story: Sexyy Red's 'SkeeYee' (June 9, 2023; produced by Tay Keith, DJ Meech, BanBwoi) went viral pre-official release via a hair-whipping move tied to the ad-lib. No single named dance creator surfaced in primary journalism.
timeline: Pre-release virality -> June 9 2023 release -> No. 1 on inaugural TikTok Billboard Top 50 (Sept 16, 2023).
fun_facts: Sexyy Red placed four songs on the first TikTok Billboard Top 50. Ranked Rolling Stone's Best Rap Song of 2023.

name: The Original Harlem Shake (the real dance, 1981)
originator: Albert Leopold 'Al B.' Boyce — uncredited Black creator
originator_story: Albert Leopold 'Al B.' Boyce (also known as Ali Saadat / Al Cisco) created the original Harlem Shake in 1981, first called 'The Albee' after him; he performed it during breaks at the Entertainer's Basketball Classic at Rucker Park. His mother Sandra Boyce said the move grew from her own dancing ('That was my dance. He just added the shake'). Popularized by the Crazy Boyz crew (Maurice 'Motion' Strayhorn, Jesse 'Smiley' Rutland, Kirkland 'Dirty Kirt' Young, Joseph 'No Bones' Collins) and mainstreamed by G. Dep's 2001 'Let's Get It' video. Al B. refused to sell the rights and died of heart failure in 2006, age 43, uncredited and unprofited. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: this is entirely separate from the 2013 Baauer internet meme (already cataloged as harlem-shake-meme).
song: (no single song — street dance performed at Rucker Park, Harlem)
timeline: 1981 Rucker Park creation -> popularized by Crazy Boyz crew -> G. Dep 'Let's Get It' (2001) video mainstream -> Bow Wow and Jadakiss videos -> 2006 'Chicken Noodle Soup' revival.
fun_facts: Al B. died in 2006 without profiting — a textbook case of an uncredited Black creator. Some sources cite Ethiopian Eskista as an influence.

name: The Black Bottom
originator: Emerged among African Americans (rural South, c.1907); Alberta Hunter/Perry Bradford/Billy Pierce contested as originators; mainstreamed by white performers
originator_story: The Black Bottom emerged among African Americans in the rural South (documented as early as 1907, with New Orleans roots), derived from 'the Echo.' Blues singer Alberta Hunter claimed to have introduced it c.1925; songwriter Perry Bradford published 'Original Black Bottom Dance' (1919/1926); choreographer Billy Pierce is credited on some sheet music. It was mainstreamed — and arguably appropriated — by white performer Ann Pennington in George White's Scandals of 1926. A documented pattern of Black originators eclipsed by white Broadway popularizers.
song: The Black Bottom (from George White's Scandals, 1926; also Alberta Hunter and various)
timeline: c.1907 rural South emergence -> 1919-26 Northern migration -> Ann Pennington's Scandals of 1926 -> overtook the Charleston as No. 1 social dance.
fun_facts: Documented appropriation of a Black folk dance by white Broadway performers.

name: Cotton Eye Joe
originator: African American folk song (pre-1865); no single dance originator; Rednex's 1994 cover created the modern line-dance craze
originator_story: The folk song predates the Civil War (documented by folklorist Dorothy Scarborough, 1925, tracing it to enslaved Black musicians on Southern plantations). No single dance originator — it was a square-dance/partner-dance tradition. Rednex's 1994 Swedish Eurodance cover (producer Pat Reiniz) created the modern line-dance craze.
song: Cotton Eye Joe (Rednex, 1994; folk song origin pre-1865)
timeline: Pre-1865 folk song -> square-dance tradition -> Rednex 1994 cover -> No. 1 across Europe, No. 25 US -> sports-arena staple.
fun_facts: In 2021 the Guelph Storm (OHL) stopped playing it over alleged racist origins.

name: Hokey Pokey / Hokey Cokey
originator: Genuinely contested (Larry LaPrise 1949 US; Jimmy Kennedy 1942 UK; Al Tabor 1942; Robert Degan 1944)
originator_story: Genuinely contested origins: US copyright (1950) held by Larry LaPrise with Charles Macak and Tafit Baker (~1949, Sun Valley Idaho skiers); British songwriter Jimmy Kennedy published 'The Hokey Cokey' in 1942 (for Canadian troops in London); Al Tabor wrote a 'Hokey Pokey' in wartime London (1942); Robert Degan & Joe Brier recorded a version in Scranton PA (1944/1946) and sued LaPrise — settled as joint owners. WWII veterans told the AP (1996) they danced it in England before LaPrise's claim. No single originator.
song: Hokey Pokey (Larry LaPrise / Jimmy Kennedy / multiple competing artists)
timeline: 1942 UK wartime versions -> 1949 LaPrise recording -> copyright dispute settled as joint ownership -> Ray Anthony charting version 1953 (#13).
fun_facts: Patricia Quinn (Rocky Horror's Magenta) noted only the Hokey Pokey similarly instructs its own dance.

name: Dalla Dalla (ITZY)
originator: Euanflow (ALiEN Dance Studio) with Miu Kim and Ash
originator_story: ITZY's debut 'Dalla Dalla' (Feb 2019, JYP) was choreographed by Euanflow of ALiEN Dance Studio with Miu Kim and Ash, per ALiEN's official demo video credits. The original ALiEN demo was notably more intense than the final performed version.
timeline: Feb 2019 debut -> major K-pop dance challenge.
fun_facts: The original ALiEN demo circulated widely; the final performance was toned down.

name: Wannabe (ITZY)
originator: Leejung Lee (The Black Label / formerly YGX & Just Jerk)
originator_story: ITZY's 'Wannabe' (2020, JYP) was choreographed by Leejung Lee, who created the iconic 'shoulder dance' (Ryujin's center move). Lee rose to fame on Street Woman Fighter (2021) and later choreographed for KPop Demon Hunters (2025).
timeline: 2020 release -> Ryujin's 'shoulder dance' becomes iconic K-pop challenge.
fun_facts: Leejung Lee is one of Korea's most documented individual choreographers.

name: Lalisa / Money (Lisa)
originator: Leejung Lee (credited) with Kiel Tutin (co-choreography)
originator_story: Lisa's 'Money' (Sept 2021, from Lalisa EP) was choreographed by Leejung Lee (credited in Wikipedia liner notes) with Kiel Tutin co-choreographing. In April 2023 Leejung Lee was harassed by some BLACKPINK fans for writing 'seeing you performing my choreography' about Lisa at Coachella; Kiel Tutin publicly defended her. 'Money' became the first K-pop track by a solo artist to reach 1B Spotify streams (Sept 21, 2023, per Guinness World Records).
timeline: Sept 2021 release -> TikTok/Reels dance covers -> 1B Spotify streams Sept 2023 (Guinness record) -> 1B YouTube views May 2024.
fun_facts: Credit dispute: Leejung Lee harassed by some fans; Kiel Tutin defended her.

name: Pink Venom (BLACKPINK)
originator: Kiel Tutin, Sienna Lalau, Lee Jung (YGX), Taryn Cheng (YGX)
originator_story: Per the official MTV VMAs announcement (Best Choreography, Sept 13, 2023): 'Pink Venom choreography by Kiel Tutin, Sienna Lalau, Lee Jung (YGX), Taryn Cheng (YGX).' A multi-choreographer credit, all four named.
timeline: Aug 2022 release -> VMA Best Choreography win Sept 2023.

name: Shut Down (BLACKPINK)
originator: Kiel Tutin, Taryn Cheng, Lee Jeong (Ri Jeong), Kyle Hanagami (YGX team)
originator_story: Per Wikipedia and allkpop, 'Shut Down' was choreographed by Kiel Tutin, Taryn Cheng, Lee Jeong (Ri Jeong), and Kyle Hanagami (YGX team). The choreographers said they 'emphasized BLACKPINK's unique strong hip-hop swag.'
timeline: Sept 2022 release -> dance challenge.

name: Armageddon (aespa)
originator: Renan (1MILLION Dance Studio)
originator_story: aespa's 'Armageddon' (2024) was choreographed by Renan of 1MILLION Dance Studio, who confirmed a move was changed for member Karina after the original was too intense. Renan features in the official aespa tutorial.
timeline: 2024 release -> dance challenge -> Renan featured in official tutorial.

name: Drama (aespa)
originator: REDLIC (Han Ha-eun) and Bada Lee
originator_story: aespa's 'Drama' (Nov 2023) was choreographed by REDLIC (Han Ha-eun) and Bada Lee (of Street Woman Fighter 2 fame), who confirmed her involvement on Instagram.
timeline: Nov 2023 release -> dance challenge.

name: Super Lady ((G)I-DLE)
originator: Kirsten Dodgen
originator_story: Kirsten Dodgen ('Kirsten') is confirmed as choreographer of (G)I-DLE's 'Super Lady' (Jan 2024), via UPI and her own TikTok.
timeline: Jan 2024 release -> dance challenge.

name: Tomboy ((G)I-DLE)
originator: Possibly Queena Yang (self-attributed, lower confidence)
originator_story: The 'Tomboy' (2022) point choreography (finger-gun/'ILY' hand sign) is self-attributed to Queena Yang on Instagram — lower confidence, not corroborated by major outlets. A move was altered during filming because member Shuhua couldn't form the hand shape.
timeline: 2022 release -> viral point-dance challenge.
fun_facts: Attribution lower-confidence; needs primary journalism corroboration.

name: IDOL (BTS)
originator: Son Sung-deuk (with Rie Hata); incorporates Korean traditional dance
originator_story: BTS's 'IDOL' (Aug 2018) choreography was led by Son Sung-deuk (BTS's longtime performance director, Big Hit since 2009) with input from Rie Hata (leader of Queen of Swag). The routine incorporates Korean traditional dance elements (shouts '얼쑤'/'eolssu' and '지화자'/'jihwaja' reflect traditional Korean performance culture).
timeline: Aug 2018 release -> global challenge; incorporates traditional Korean dance forms.
fun_facts: Son Sung-deuk oversees BTS's full performance direction; members occasionally self-choreograph.
